I am betray'd, by keeping company
With men like men of inconstancy.
When shall you see me write a thing in rhyme?
Or groan for love? or spend a minute's time
In pruning me? When shall you hear that I
Will praise a hand, a foot, a face, an eye,
A gait, a state, a brow, a breast, a waist,
A leg, a limb?

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
Soft! whither away so fast?
A true man or a thief that gallops so?

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: I post from love: good lover, let me go.
Enter JAQUENETTA and COSTARD

units.speaker: JAQUENETTA
units.text: God bless the King!

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: What present hast thou there?

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text: Some certain treason.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: What makes treason here?

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text: Nay, it makes nothing, sir.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
If it mar nothing neither,
The treason and you go in peace away together.

units.speaker: JAQUENETTA
units.text:
I beseech your grace, let this letter be read:
Our parson misdoubts it; 'twas treason, he said.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Biron, read it over.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Where hadst thou it?

units.speaker: JAQUENETTA
units.text: Of Costard.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Where hadst thou it?

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text: Of Dun Adramadio, Dun Adramadio.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: How now! what is in you? why dost thou tear it?

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: A toy, my liege, a toy: your grace needs not fear it.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text:
It did move him to passion, and therefore let's hear
it.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: It is Biron's writing, and here is his name.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
[To COSTARD] Ah, you whoreson loggerhead! you were
born to do me shame.
Guilty, my lord, guilty! I confess, I confess.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: What?

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
That you three fools lack'd me fool to make up the
mess:
He, he, and you, and you, my liege, and I,
Are pick-purses in love, and we deserve to die.
O, dismiss this audience, and I shall tell you more.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Now the number is even.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
True, true; we are four.
Will these turtles be gone?

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Hence, sirs; away!

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text: Walk aside the true folk, and let the traitors stay.
Exeunt COSTARD and JAQUENETTA

units.speaker: BIRON
FERDINAND
units.text: What, did these rent lines show some love of thine?

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
Did they, quoth you? Who sees the heavenly Rosaline,
That, like a rude and savage man of Inde,
At the first opening of the gorgeous east,
Bows not his vassal head and strucken blind
Kisses the base ground with obedient breast?
What peremptory eagle-sighted eye
Dares look upon the heaven of her brow,
That is not blinded by her majesty?

units.speaker: FERDINAND
BIRON
units.text:
My eyes are then no eyes, nor I Biron:
O, but for my love, day would turn to night!
Of all complexions the cull'd sovereignty
Do meet, as at a fair, in her fair cheek,
Where several worthies make one dignity,
Where nothing wants that want itself doth seek.
Lend me the flourish of all gentle tongues,—
Fie, painted rhetoric! O, she needs it not:
To things of sale a seller's praise belongs,
She passes praise; then praise too short doth blot.
A wither'd hermit, five-score winters worn,
Might shake off fifty, looking in her eye:
Beauty doth varnish age, as if new-born,
And gives the crutch the cradle's infancy:
O, 'tis the sun that maketh all things shine.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: By heaven, thy love is black as ebony.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
Is ebony like her? O wood divine!
A wife of such wood were felicity.
O, who can give an oath? where is a book?
That I may swear beauty doth beauty lack,
If that she learn not of her eye to look:
No face is fair that is not full so black.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
BIRON
units.text:
Devils soonest tempt, resembling spirits of light.
O, if in black my lady's brows be deck'd,
It mourns that painting and usurping hair
Should ravish doters with a false aspect;
And therefore is she born to make black fair.
Her favour turns the fashion of the days,
For native blood is counted painting now;
And therefore red, that would avoid dispraise,
Paints itself black, to imitate her brow.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: To look like her are chimney-sweepers black.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text: And since her time are colliers counted bright.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: And Ethiopes of their sweet complexion crack.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Dark needs no candles now, for dark is light.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
Your mistresses dare never come in rain,
For fear their colours should be wash'd away.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
'Twere good, yours did; for, sir, to tell you plain,
I'll find a fairer face not wash'd to-day.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: I'll prove her fair, or talk till doomsday here.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: No devil will fright thee then so much as she.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: I never knew man hold vile stuff so dear.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text: Look, here's thy love: my foot and her face see.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
O, if the streets were paved with thine eyes,
Her feet were much too dainty for such tread!

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text:
O, vile! then, as she goes, what upward lies
The street should see as she walk'd overhead.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: But what of this? are we not all in love?

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Nothing so sure; and thereby all forsworn.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
Then leave this chat; and, good Biron, now prove
Our loving lawful, and our faith not torn.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Ay, marry, there; some flattery for this evil.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text:
O, some authority how to proceed;
Some tricks, some quillets, how to cheat the devil.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Some salve for perjury.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
'Tis more than need.
Have at you, then, affection's men at arms.
Consider what you first did swear unto,
To fast, to study, and to see no woman;
Flat treason 'gainst the kingly state of youth.
Say, can you fast? your stomachs are too young;
And abstinence engenders maladies.
O, we have made a vow to study, lords,
And in that vow we have forsworn our books.
For when would you, my liege, or you, or you,
In leaden contemplation have found out
Such fiery numbers as the prompting eyes
Of beauty's tutors have enrich'd you with?
Other slow arts entirely keep the brain;
And therefore, finding barren practisers,
Scarce show a harvest of their heavy toil:
But love, first learned in a lady's eyes,
Lives not alone immured in the brain;
But, with the motion of all elements,
Courses as swift as thought in every power,
And gives to every power a double power,
Above their functions and their offices.
It adds a precious seeing to the eye;
A lover's eyes will gaze an eagle blind;
A lover's ear will hear the lowest sound,
When the suspicious head of theft is stopp'd:
Love's feeling is more soft and sensible
Than are the tender horns of cockl'd snails;
Love's tongue proves dainty Bacchus gross in taste:
For valour, is not Love a Hercules,
Still climbing trees in the Hesperides?
Subtle as Sphinx; as sweet and musical
As bright Apollo's lute, strung with his hair:
And when Love speaks, the voice of all the gods
Makes heaven drowsy with the harmony.
Never durst poet touch a pen to write
Until his ink were temper'd with Love's sighs;
O, then his lines would ravish savage ears
And plant in tyrants mild humility.
From women's eyes this doctrine I derive:
They sparkle still the right Promethean fire;
They are the books, the arts, the academes,
That show, contain and nourish all the world:
Else none at all in ought proves excellent.
Then fools you were these women to forswear,
Or keeping what is sworn, you will prove fools.
For wisdom's sake, a word that all men love,
Or for love's sake, a word that loves all men,
Or for men's sake, the authors of these women,
Or women's sake, by whom we men are men,
Let us once lose our oaths to find ourselves,
Or else we lose ourselves to keep our oaths.
It is religion to be thus forsworn,
For charity itself fulfills the law,
And who can sever love from charity?

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Saint Cupid, then! and, soldiers, to the field!

units.speaker: BIRON
LONGAVILLE
units.text:
Now to plain-dealing; lay these glozes by:
Shall we resolve to woo these girls of France?

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
And win them too: therefore let us devise
Some entertainment for them in their tents.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
First, from the park let us conduct them thither;
Then homeward every man attach the hand
Of his fair mistress: in the afternoon
We will with some strange pastime solace them,
Such as the shortness of the time can shape;
For revels, dances, masks and merry hours
Forerun fair Love, strewing her way with flowers.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
Away, away! no time shall be omitted
That will betime, and may by us be fitted.

units.speaker: BIRON

units.text: Exeunt
Enter HOLOFERNES, SIR NATHANIEL, and DULL

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: Satis quod sufficit.

units.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
units.text:
I praise God for you, sir: your reasons at dinner
have been sharp and sententious; pleasant without
scurrility, witty without affection, audacious without
impudency, learned without opinion, and strange with-
out heresy. I did converse this quondam day with
a companion of the King's, who is intituled, nomi-
nated, or called, Don Adriano de Armado.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text:
Novi hominem tanquam te: his humour is lofty, his
discourse peremptory, his tongue filed, his eye
ambitious, his gait majestical, and his general
behavior vain, ridiculous, and thrasonical. He is
too picked, too spruce, too affected, too odd, as it
were, too peregrinate, as I may call it.

units.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
units.text: A most singular and choice epithet.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text:
He draweth out the thread of his verbosity finer
than the staple of his argument. I abhor such
fanatical phantasimes, such insociable and
point-devise companions; such rackers of
orthography, as to speak dout, sine b, when he should
say doubt; det, when he should pronounce debt,—d,
e, b, t, not d, e, t: he clepeth a calf, cauf;
half, hauf; neighbour vocatur nebor; neigh
abbreviated ne. This is abhominable,—which he
would call abbominable: it insinuateth me of
insanie: ne intelligis, domine? to make frantic,
lunatic.

units.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
units.text: Laus Deo, bone intelligo.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text:
Bon, bon, for bene! Priscian a little scratch'd,
'twill serve.

units.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
units.text: Videsne quis venit?

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: Video, et gaudeo.
Enter DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO, MOTH, and COSTARD

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text: Chirrah!

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: Quare chirrah, not sirrah?

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text: Men of peace, well encountered.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: Most military sir, salutation.

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text:
[Aside to COSTARD] They have been at a great feast
of languages, and stolen the scraps.

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
O, they have lived long on the alms-basket of words.
I marvel thy master hath not eaten thee for a word;
for thou art not so long by the head as
honorificabilitudinitatibus: thou art easier
swallowed than a flap-dragon.

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text: Peace! the peal begins.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text: [To HOLOFERNES] Monsieur, are you not lettered?

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text:
Yes, yes; he teaches boys the hornbook. What is a,
b, spelt backward, with the horn on his head?

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: Ba, pueritia, with a horn added.

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text:
Ba, most silly sheep with a horn. You hear his
learning.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: Quis, quis, thou consonant?

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text:
The third of the five vowels, if you repeat them; or
the fifth, if I.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: I will repeat them,—a, e, i,—

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text: The sheep: the other two concludes it,—o, u.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
Now, by the salt wave of the Mediterraneum, a sweet
touch, a quick venue of wit! snip, snap, quick and
home! it rejoiceth my intellect: true wit!

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text: Offered by a child to an old man; which is wit-old.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: What is the figure? what is the figure?

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text: Horns.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: Thou disputest like an infant: go, whip thy gig.

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text:
Lend me your horn to make one, and I will whip about
your infamy manu cita.—a gig of a cuckold's horn.

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
An I had but one penny in the world, thou shouldst
have it to buy gingerbread: hold, there is the very
remuneration I had of thy master, thou halfpenny
purse of wit, thou pigeon-egg of discretion. O, an
the heavens were so pleased that thou wert but my
bastard, what a joyful father wouldst thou make me!
Go to; thou hast it ad dunghill, at the fingers'
ends, as they say.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: O, I smell false Latin; dunghill for unguem.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
Arts-man, preambulate, we will be singled from the
barbarous. Do you not educate youth at the
charge-house on the top of the mountain?

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: Or mons, the hill.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text: At your sweet pleasure, for the mountain.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: I do, sans question.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
Sir, it is the king's most sweet pleasure and
affection to congratulate the Princess at her
pavilion in the posteriors of this day, which the
rude multitude call the afternoon.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text:
The posterior of the day, most generous sir, is
liable, congruent and measurable for the afternoon:
the word is well culled, chose, sweet and apt, I do
assure you, sir, I do assure.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
Sir, the king is a noble gentleman, and my familiar,
I do assure ye, very good friend: for what is
inward between us, let it pass. I do beseech thee,
remember thy courtesy; I beseech thee, apparel thy
head: and among other important and most serious
designs, and of great import indeed, too, but let
that pass: for I must tell thee, it will please his
grace, by the world, sometime to lean upon my poor
shoulder, and with his royal finger, thus, dally
with my excrement, with my mustachio; but, sweet
heart, let that pass. By the world, I recount no
fable: some certain special honours it pleaseth his
greatness to impart to Armado, a soldier, a man of
travel, that hath seen the world; but let that pass.
The very all of all is,—but, sweet heart, I do
implore secrecy, —that the king would have me
present the princess, sweet chuck, with some
delightful ostentation, or show, or pageant, or
antique, or firework. Now, understanding that the
curate and your sweet self are good at such
eruptions and sudden breaking out of mirth, as it
were, I have acquainted you withal, to the end to
crave your assistance.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text:
Sir, you shall present before her the Nine Worthies.
Sir Nathaniel, as concerning some entertainment of
time, some
show in the posterior of this day, to be rendered by
our assistants, at the king's command, and this most
gallant, illustrate, and learned gentleman, before
the princess; I say none so fit as to present the
Nine Worthies.

units.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
units.text: Where will you find men worthy enough to present them?

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text:
Joshua, yourself; this gallant gentleman,
Judas Maccabaeus; this swain, because of his great
limb or joint, shall pass Pompey the Great; the
page, Hercules,—

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
Pardon, sir; error: he is not quantity enough for
that Worthy's thumb: he is not so big as the end of his
club.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text:
Shall I have audience? he shall present Hercules in
minority: his enter and exit shall be strangling a
snake; and I will have an apology for that purpose.

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text:
An excellent device! so, if any of the audience
hiss, you may cry 'Well done, Hercules! now thou
crushest the snake!' that is the way to make an
offence gracious, though few have the grace to do it.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text: For the rest of the Worthies?—

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: I will play three myself.

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text: Thrice-worthy gentleman!

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text: Shall I tell you a thing?

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: We attend.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
We will have, if this fadge not, an antic. I
beseech you, follow.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text:
Via, goodman Dull! thou hast spoken no word all this
while.

units.speaker: DULL
units.text: Nor understood none neither, sir.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: Allons! we will employ thee.

units.speaker: DULL
units.text:
I'll make one in a dance, or so; or I will play
On the tabour to the Worthies, and let them dance the
hay.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: Most dull, honest Dull! To our sport, away!
Exeunt
Enter the PRINCESS, KATHARINE, ROSALINE, and MARIA

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
Sweet hearts, we shall be rich ere we depart,
If fairings come thus plentifully in:
A lady wall'd about with diamonds!
Look you what I have from the loving king.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: Madame, came nothing else along with that?

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
Nothing but this! yes, as much love in rhyme
As would be cramm'd up in a sheet of paper,
Writ o' both sides the leaf, margent and all,
That he was fain to seal on Cupid's name.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
That was the way to make his godhead wax,
For he hath been five thousand years a boy.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: Ay, and a shrewd unhappy gallows too.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
You'll ne'er be friends with him; a' kill'd your
sister.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text:
He made her melancholy, sad, and heavy;
And so she died: had she been light, like you,
Of such a merry, nimble, stirring spirit,
She might ha' been a grandam ere she died:
And so may you; for a light heart lives long.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: What's your dark meaning, mouse, of this light word?

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: A light condition in a beauty dark.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: We need more light to find your meaning out.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text:
You'll mar the light by taking it in snuff;
Therefore I'll darkly end the argument.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: Look what you do, you do it still i' the dark.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: So do not you, for you are a light wench.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: Indeed I weigh not you, and therefore light.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: You weigh me not? O, that's you care not for me.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: Great reason; for 'past cure is still past care.'

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
Well bandied both; a set of wit well play'd.
But Rosaline, you have a favour too:
Who sent it? and what is it?

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
I would you knew:
An if my face were but as fair as yours,
My favour were as great; be witness this.
Nay, I have verses too, I thank Biron:
The numbers true; and, were the numbering too,
I were the fairest goddess on the ground:
I am compared to twenty thousand fairs.
O, he hath drawn my picture in his letter!

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Any thing like?

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: Much in the letters; nothing in the praise.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Beauteous as ink; a good conclusion.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: Fair as a text B in a copy-book.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
'Ware pencils, ho! let me not die your debtor,
My red dominical, my golden letter:
O, that your face were not so full of O's!

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: A pox of that jest! and I beshrew all shrows.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: But, Katharine, what was sent to you from fair Dumain?

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: Madam, this glove.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Did he not send you twain?

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text:
Yes, madam, and moreover
Some thousand verses of a faithful lover,
A huge translation of hypocrisy,
Vilely compiled, profound simplicity.

units.speaker: MARIA
units.text:
This and these pearls to me sent Longaville:
The letter is too long by half a mile.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
I think no less. Dost thou not wish in heart
The chain were longer and the letter short?

units.speaker: MARIA
units.text: Ay, or I would these hands might never part.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: We are wise girls to mock our lovers so.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
They are worse fools to purchase mocking so.
That same Biron I'll torture ere I go:
O that I knew he were but in by the week!
How I would make him fawn and beg and seek
And wait the season and observe the times
And spend his prodigal wits in bootless rhymes
And shape his service wholly to my hests
And make him proud to make me proud that jests!
So pair-taunt-like would I o'ersway his state
That he should be my fool and I his fate.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
None are so surely caught, when they are catch'd,
As wit turn'd fool: folly, in wisdom hatch'd,
Hath wisdom's warrant and the help of school
And wit's own grace to grace a learned fool.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
The blood of youth burns not with such excess
As gravity's revolt to wantonness.

units.speaker: MARIA
units.text:
Folly in fools bears not so strong a note
As foolery in the wise, when wit doth dote;
Since all the power thereof it doth apply
To prove, by wit, worth in simplicity.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Here comes Boyet, and mirth is in his face.
Enter BOYET

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: O, I am stabb'd with laughter! Where's her grace?

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Thy news Boyet?

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text:
Prepare, madam, prepare!
Arm, wenches, arm! encounters mounted are
Against your peace: Love doth approach disguised,
Armed in arguments; you'll be surprised:
Muster your wits; stand in your own defence;
Or hide your heads like cowards, and fly hence.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
Saint Denis to Saint Cupid! What are they
That charge their breath against us? say, scout, say.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text:
Under the cool shade of a sycamore
I thought to close mine eyes some half an hour;
When, lo! to interrupt my purposed rest,
Toward that shade I might behold addrest
The king and his companions: warily
I stole into a neighbour thicket by,
And overheard what you shall overhear,
That, by and by, disguised they will be here.
Their herald is a pretty knavish page,
That well by heart hath conn'd his embassage:
Action and accent did they teach him there;
'Thus must thou speak,' and 'thus thy body bear:'
And ever and anon they made a doubt
Presence majestical would put him out,
'For,' quoth the king, 'an angel shalt thou see;
Yet fear not thou, but speak audaciously.'
The boy replied, 'An angel is not evil;
I should have fear'd her had she been a devil.'
With that, all laugh'd and clapp'd him on the shoulder,
Making the bold wag by their praises bolder:
One rubb'd his elbow thus, and fleer'd and swore
A better speech was never spoke before;
Another, with his finger and his thumb,
Cried, 'Via! we will do't, come what will come;'
The third he caper'd, and cried, 'All goes well;'
The fourth turn'd on the toe, and down he fell.
With that, they all did tumble on the ground,
With such a zealous laughter, so profound,
That in this spleen ridiculous appears,
To cheque their folly, passion's solemn tears.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: But what, but what, come they to visit us?

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text:
They do, they do: and are apparell'd thus.
Like Muscovites or Russians, as I guess.
Their purpose is to parle, to court and dance;
And every one his love-feat will advance
Unto his several mistress, which they'll know
By favours several which they did bestow.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
ROSALINE
units.text: Come on, then; wear the favours most in sight.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: But in this changing what is your intent?

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
The effect of my intent is to cross theirs:
They do it but in mocking merriment;
And mock for mock is only my intent.
Their several counsels they unbosom shall
To loves mistook, and so be mock'd withal
Upon the next occasion that we meet,
With visages displayed, to talk and greet.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: But shall we dance, if they desire to't?

units.speaker: PRINCESS
BOYET
units.text:
Why, that contempt will kill the speaker's heart,
And quite divorce his memory from his part.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
Therefore I do it; and I make no doubt
The rest will ne'er come in, if he be out
There's no such sport as sport by sport o'erthrown,
To make theirs ours and ours none but our own:
So shall we stay, mocking intended game,
And they, well mock'd, depart away with shame.
Trumpets sound within

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: The trumpet sounds: be mask'd; the maskers come.
Enter Blackamoors with music; MOTH; FERDINAND, BIRON, LONGAVILLE, and DUMAIN, in Russian habits, and masked

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text: All hail, the richest beauties on the earth!—

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: Beauties no richer than rich taffeta.

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text: A holy parcel of the fairest dames.

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text: That ever turn'd their—backs—to mortal views!

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: [Aside to MOTH] Their eyes, villain, their eyes!

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text: That ever turn'd their eyes to mortal views!—Out—

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: True; out indeed.

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text:
Out of your favours, heavenly spirits, vouchsafe
Not to behold—

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: [Aside to MOTH] Once to behold, rogue.

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text:
Once to behold with your sun-beamed eyes,
—with your sun-beamed eyes—

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text:
They will not answer to that epithet;
You were best call it 'daughter-beamed eyes.'

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text: They do not mark me, and that brings me out.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Is this your perfectness? be gone, you rogue!
Exit MOTH

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
What would these strangers? know their minds, Boyet:
If they do speak our language, 'tis our will:
That some plain man recount their purposes
Know what they would.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: What would you with the Princess?

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Nothing but peace and gentle visitation.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: What would they, say they?

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: Nothing but peace and gentle visitation.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: Why, that they have; and bid them so be gone.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: She says, you have it, and you may be gone.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
Say to her, we have measured many miles
To tread a measure with her on this grass.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text:
They say, that they have measured many a mile
To tread a measure with you on this grass.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
It is not so. Ask them how many inches
Is in one mile: if they have measured many,
The measure then of one is easily told.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text:
If to come hither you have measured miles,
And many miles, the princess bids you tell
How many inches doth fill up one mile.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Tell her, we measure them by weary steps.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: She hears herself.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
How many weary steps,
Of many weary miles you have o'ergone,
Are number'd in the travel of one mile?

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
We number nothing that we spend for you:
Our duty is so rich, so infinite,
That we may do it still without accompt.
Vouchsafe to show the sunshine of your face,
That we, like savages, may worship it.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: My face is but a moon, and clouded too.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
Blessed are clouds, to do as such clouds do!
Vouchsafe, bright moon, and these thy stars, to shine,
Those clouds removed, upon our watery eyne.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
O vain petitioner! beg a greater matter;
Thou now request'st but moonshine in the water.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
Then, in our measure do but vouchsafe one change.
Thou bid'st me beg: this begging is not strange.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: Play, music, then! Nay, you must do it soon.
Music plays

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: Not yet! no dance! Thus change I like the moon.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Will you not dance? How come you thus estranged?

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: You took the moon at full, but now she's changed.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
Yet still she is the moon, and I the man.
The music plays; vouchsafe some motion to it.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: Our ears vouchsafe it.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: But your legs should do it.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
Since you are strangers and come here by chance,
We'll not be nice: take hands. We will not dance.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Why take we hands, then?

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
Only to part friends:
Curtsy, sweet hearts; and so the measure ends.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: More measure of this measure; be not nice.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: We can afford no more at such a price.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Prize you yourselves: what buys your company?

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: Your absence only.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: That can never be.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
Then cannot we be bought: and so, adieu;
Twice to your visor, and half once to you.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: If you deny to dance, let's hold more chat.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: In private, then.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: I am best pleased with that.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: White-handed mistress, one sweet word with thee.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Honey, and milk, and sugar; there is three.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
Nay then, two treys, and if you grow so nice,
Metheglin, wort, and malmsey: well run, dice!
There's half-a-dozen sweets.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
Seventh sweet, adieu:
Since you can cog, I'll play no more with you.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: One word in secret.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Let it not be sweet.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Thou grievest my gall.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Gall! bitter.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Therefore meet.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Will you vouchsafe with me to change a word?

units.speaker: MARIA
units.text: Name it.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Fair lady,—

units.speaker: MARIA
units.text:
Say you so? Fair lord,—
Take that for your fair lady.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text:
Please it you,
As much in private, and I'll bid adieu.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: What, was your vizard made without a tongue?

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text: I know the reason, lady, why you ask.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: O for your reason! quickly, sir; I long.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text:
You have a double tongue within your mask,
And would afford my speechless vizard half.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: Veal, quoth the Dutchman. Is not "veal" a calf?

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text: A calf, fair lady!

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: No, a fair lord calf.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text: Let's part the word.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text:
No, I'll not be your half
Take all, and wean it; it may prove an ox.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text:
Look, how you butt yourself in these sharp mocks!
Will you give horns, chaste lady? do not so.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: Then die a calf, before your horns do grow.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text: One word in private with you, ere I die.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: Bleat softly then; the butcher hears you cry.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text:
The tongues of mocking wenches are as keen
As is the razor's edge invisible,
Cutting a smaller hair than may be seen,
Above the sense of sense; so sensible
Seemeth their conference; their conceits have wings
Fleeter than arrows, bullets, wind, thought, swifter
things.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: Not one word more, my maids; break off, break off.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: By heaven, all dry-beaten with pure scoff!

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Farewell, mad wenches; you have simple wits.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Twenty adieus, my frozen Muscovits.
Exeunt FERDINAND, Lords, and Blackamoors

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Are these the breed of wits so wonder'd at?

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: Tapers they are, with your sweet breaths puff'd out.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: Well-liking wits they have; gross, gross; fat, fat.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
O poverty in wit, kingly-poor flout!
Will they not, think you, hang themselves tonight?
Or ever, but in vizards, show their faces?
This pert Biron was out of countenance quite.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
O, they were all in lamentable cases!
The king was weeping-ripe for a good word.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Biron did swear himself out of all suit.

units.speaker: MARIA
units.text:
Dumain was at my service, and his sword:
Non point, quoth I; my servant straight was mute.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text:
Lord Longaville said, I came o'er his heart;
And trow you what he called me?

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Qualm, perhaps.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: Yes, in good faith.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Go, sickness as thou art!

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
Well, better wits have worn plain statute-caps.
But will you hear? the king is my love sworn.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: And quick Biron hath plighted faith to me.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: And Longaville was for my service born.

units.speaker: MARIA
units.text: Dumain is mine, as sure as bark on tree.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text:
Madam, and pretty mistresses, give ear:
Immediately they will again be here
In their own shapes; for it can never be
They will digest this harsh indignity.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Will they return?

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text:
They will, they will, God knows,
And leap for joy, though they are lame with blows:
Therefore change favours; and, when they repair,
Blow like sweet roses in this summer air.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: How blow? how blow? speak to be understood.

units.speaker: BOYET
PRINCESS
units.text:
Avaunt, perplexity! What shall we do,
If they return in their own shapes to woo?

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
Good madam, if by me you'll be advised,
Let's, mock them still, as well known as disguised:
Let us complain to them what fools were here,
Disguised like Muscovites, in shapeless gear;
And wonder what they were and to what end
Their shallow shows and prologue vilely penn'd
And their rough carriage so ridiculous,
Should be presented at our tent to us.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: Ladies, withdraw: the gallants are at hand.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Whip to our tents, as roes run o'er land.
Exeunt PRINCESS, ROSALINE, KATHARINE, and MARIA
Re-enter FERDINAND, BIRON, LONGAVILLE, and DUMAIN, in their proper habits

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Fair sir, God save you! Where's the princess?

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text:
Gone to her tent. Please it your majesty
Command me any service to her thither?

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: That she vouchsafe me audience for one word.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: I will; and so will she, I know, my lord.
Exit

units.speaker: BIRON
FERDINAND
units.text:
A blister on his sweet tongue, with my heart,
That put Armado's page out of his part!

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
See where it comes! Behavior, what wert thou
Till this madman show'd thee? and what art thou now?
Re-enter the PRINCESS, ushered by BOYET, ROSALINE, MARIA, and KATHARINE

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: All hail, sweet madam, and fair time of day!

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: "Fair" in "all hail" is foul, as I conceive.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Construe my speeches better, if you may.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Then wish me better; I will give you leave.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
We came to visit you, and purpose now
To lead you to our court; vouchsafe it then.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
This field shall hold me; and so hold your vow:
Nor God, nor I, delights in perjured men.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
Rebuke me not for that which you provoke:
The virtue of your eye must break my oath.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
You nickname virtue; vice you should have spoke;
For virtue's office never breaks men's troth.
Now by my maiden honour, yet as pure
As the unsullied lily, I protest,
A world of torments though I should endure,
I would not yield to be your house's guest;
So much I hate a breaking cause to be
Of heavenly oaths, vow'd with integrity.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
O, you have lived in desolation here,
Unseen, unvisited, much to our shame.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
FERDINAND
units.text: How, madam! Russians!

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
Ay, in truth, my lord;
Trim gallants, full of courtship and of state.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
Madam, speak true. It is not so, my lord:
My lady, to the manner of the days,
In courtesy gives undeserving praise.
We four indeed confronted were with four
In Russian habit: here they stay'd an hour,
And talk'd apace; and in that hour, my lord,
They did not bless us with one happy word.
I dare not call them fools; but this I think,
When they are thirsty, fools would fain have drink.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
This jest is dry to me. Fair gentle sweet,
Your wit makes wise things foolish: when we greet,
With eyes best seeing, heaven's fiery eye,
By light we lose light: your capacity
Is of that nature that to your huge store
Wise things seem foolish and rich things but poor.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: This proves you wise and rich, for in my eye,—

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: I am a fool, and full of poverty.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
But that you take what doth to you belong,
It were a fault to snatch words from my tongue.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: O, I am yours, and all that I possess!

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: All the fool mine?

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: I cannot give you less.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: Which of the vizards was it that you wore?

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Where? when? what vizard? why demand you this?

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
There, then, that vizard; that superfluous case
That hid the worse and show'd the better face.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: We are descried; they'll mock us now downright.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Let us confess and turn it to a jest.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Amazed, my lord? why looks your highness sad?

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
Help, hold his brows! he'll swoon! Why look you pale?
Sea-sick, I think, coming from Muscovy.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
Thus pour the stars down plagues for perjury.
Can any face of brass hold longer out? Here stand I,
lady, dart thy skill at me; Bruise me
with scorn, confound me with a flout;
Thrust thy sharp wit quite through my ignorance;
Cut me to pieces with thy keen conceit;
And I will wish thee never more to dance,
Nor never more in Russian habit wait.
O, never will I trust to speeches penn'd,
Nor to the motion of a schoolboy's tongue,
Nor never come in vizard to my friend,
Nor woo in rhyme, like a blind harper's song!
Taffeta phrases, silken terms precise,
Three-piled hyperboles, spruce affectation,
Figures pedantical; these summer-flies
Have blown me full of maggot ostentation:
I do forswear them; and I here protest,
By this white glove;—how white the hand, God knows!—
Henceforth my wooing mind shall be express'd
In russet yeas and honest kersey noes:
And, to begin, wench,—so God help me, la!—
My love to thee is sound, sans crack or flaw.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: Sans sans, I pray you.

units.speaker: BIRON
PRINCESS
units.text: No, they are free that gave these tokens to us.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Our states are forfeit: seek not to undo us.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
It is not so; for how can this be true,
That you stand forfeit, being those that sue?

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Peace! for I will not have to do with you.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: Nor shall not, if I do as I intend.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Speak for yourselves; my wit is at an end.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
Teach us, sweet madam, for our rude transgression
Some fair excuse.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
The fairest is confession.
Were not you here but even now disguised?

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Madam, I was.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: And were you well advised?

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: I was, fair madam.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
When you then were here,
What did you whisper in your lady's ear?

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: That more than all the world I did respect her.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: When she shall challenge this, you will reject her.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Upon mine honour, no.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
Peace, peace! forbear:
Your oath once broke, you force not to forswear.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Despise me, when I break this oath of mine.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
I will: and therefore keep it. Rosaline,
What did the Russian whisper in your ear?

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
Madam, he swore that he did hold me dear
As precious eyesight, and did value me
Above this world; adding thereto moreover
That he would wed me, or else die my lover.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
God give thee joy of him! the noble lord
Most honourably doth unhold his word.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
What mean you, madam? by my life, my troth,
I never swore this lady such an oath.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
By heaven, you did; and to confirm it plain,
You gave me this: but take it, sir, again.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
My faith and this the princess I did give:
I knew her by this jewel on her sleeve.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
BIRON
units.text:
Neither of either; I remit both twain.
I see the trick on't: here was a consent,
Knowing aforehand of our merriment,
To dash it like a Christmas comedy:
Some carry-tale, some please-man, some slight zany,
Some mumble-news, some trencher-knight, some Dick,
That smiles his cheek in years and knows the trick
To make my lady laugh when she's disposed,
Told our intents before; which once disclosed,
The ladies did change favours: and then we,
Following the signs, woo'd but the sign of she.
Now, to our perjury to add more terror,
We are again forsworn, in will and error.
Much upon this it is: and might not you.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
Forestall our sport, to make us thus untrue?
Do not you know my lady's foot by the squier,
And laugh upon the apple of her eye?
And stand between her back, sir, and the fire,
Holding a trencher, jesting merrily?
You put our page out: go, you are allow'd;
Die when you will, a smock shall be your shroud.
You leer upon me, do you? there's an eye
Wounds like a leaden sword.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text:
Full merrily
Hath this brave manage, this career, been run.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Lo, he is tilting straight! Peace! I have done.
Enter COSTARD

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Welcome, pure wit! thou partest a fair fray.

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
O Lord, sir, they would know
Whether the three Worthies shall come in or no.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: What, are there but three?

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
No, sir; but it is vara fine,
For every one pursents three.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: And three times thrice is nine.

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
Not so, sir; under correction, sir; I hope it is not
so.
You cannot beg us, sir, I can assure you, sir we know
what we know:
I hope, sir, three times thrice, sir,—

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Is not nine.

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
Under correction, sir, we know whereuntil it doth
amount.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: By Jove, I always took three threes for nine.

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
O Lord, sir, it were pity you should get your living
by reckoning, sir.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: How much is it?

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
O Lord, sir, the parties themselves, the actors,
sir, will show whereuntil it doth amount: for mine
own part, I am, as they say, but to parfect one man
in one poor man, Pompion the Great, sir.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Art thou one of the Worthies?

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
It pleased them to think me worthy of Pompion the
Great: for mine own part, I know not the degree of
the Worthy, but I am to stand for him.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Go, bid them prepare.

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
We will turn it finely off, sir; we will take
some care.
Exit

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Biron, they will shame us: let them not approach.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
We are shame-proof, my lord: and tis some policy
To have one show worse than the king's and his company.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: I say they shall not come.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
Nay, my good lord, let me o'errule you now:
That sport best pleases that doth least know how:
Where zeal strives to content, and the contents
Dies in the zeal of that which it presents:
Their form confounded makes most form in mirth,
When great things labouring perish in their birth.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: A right description of our sport, my lord.
Enter DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
Anointed, I implore so much expense of thy royal
sweet breath as will utter a brace of words.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Doth this man serve God?

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Why ask you?

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: He speaks not like a man of God's making.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
That is all one, my fair, sweet, honey monarch; for,
I protest, the schoolmaster is exceeding
fantastical; too, too vain, too too vain: but we
will put it, as they say, to fortuna de la guerra.
I wish you the peace of mind, most royal couplement!
Exit

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
Here is like to be a good presence of Worthies. He
presents Hector of Troy; the swain, Pompey the
Great; the parish curate, Alexander; Armado's page,
Hercules; the pedant, Judas Maccabaeus: And if
these four Worthies in their first show thrive,
These four will change habits, and present the other
five.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: There is five in the first show.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: You are deceived; 'tis not so.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
The pedant, the braggart, the hedge-priest, the fool
and the boy:—
Abate throw at novum, and the whole world again
Cannot pick out five such, take each one in his vein.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: The ship is under sail, and here she comes amain.
Enter COSTARD, for Pompey

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text: I Pompey am,—

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: You lie, you are not he.

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text: I Pompey am,—

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: With leopard's head on knee.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
Well said, old mocker: I must needs be friends
with thee.

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text: I Pompey am, Pompey surnamed the Big—

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: The Great.

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
It is, 'Great,' sir:—
Pompey surnamed the Great;
That oft in field, with targe and shield, did make
my foe to sweat:
And travelling along this coast, I here am come by
chance,
And lay my arms before the legs of this sweet lass of
France,
If your ladyship would say, 'Thanks, Pompey,' I had
done.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Great thanks, great Pompey.

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
'Tis not so much worth; but I hope I was perfect: I
made a little fault in 'Great.'

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: My hat to a halfpenny, Pompey proves the best Worthy.

units.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
units.text:
When in the world I lived, I was the world's
commander;
By east, west, north, and south, I spread my
conquering might:
My scutcheon plain declares that I am Alisander,—

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text:
Your nose says, no, you are not for it stands too
right.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
Your nose smells 'no' in this, most tender-smelling
knight.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: The conqueror is dismay'd. Proceed, good Alexander.

units.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
units.text:
When in the world I lived, I was the world's
commander,—

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: Most true, 'tis right; you were so, Alisander.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Pompey the Great,—

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text: Your servant, and Costard.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Take away the conqueror, take away Alisander.

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
[To SIR NATHANIEL] O, sir, you have overthrown
Alisander the conqueror! You will be scraped out of
the painted cloth for this: your lion, that holds
his poll-axe sitting on a close-stool, will be given
to Ajax: he will be the ninth Worthy. A conqueror,
and afeard to speak! run away for shame, Alisander.

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
There, an't shall please you; a foolish mild man; an
honest man, look you, and soon dashed. He is a
marvellous good neighbour, faith, and a very good
bowler: but, for Alisander,—alas, you see how
'tis,—a little o'erparted. But there are Worthies
a-coming will speak their mind in some other sort.
Enter HOLOFERNES, for Judas; and MOTH, for Hercules

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text:
Great Hercules is presented by this imp,
Whose club kill'd Cerberus, that three-headed canis;
And when he was a babe, a child, a shrimp,
Thus did he strangle serpents in his manus.
Quoniam he seemeth in minority,
Ergo I come with this apology.
Keep some state in thy exit, and vanish.
MOTH retires

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: Judas I am,—

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: A Judas!

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text:
Not Iscariot, sir.
Judas I am, ycliped Maccabaeus.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Judas Maccabaeus clipt is plain Judas.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: A kissing traitor. How art thou proved Judas?

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: Judas I am,—

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: The more shame for you, Judas.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: What mean you, sir?

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: To make Judas hang himself.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: Begin, sir; you are my elder.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Well followed: Judas was hanged on an elder.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: I will not be put out of countenance.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Because thou hast no face.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: What is this?

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: A cittern-head.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: The head of a bodkin.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: A Death's face in a ring.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text: The face of an old Roman coin, scarce seen.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: The pommel of Caesar's falchion.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: The carved-bone face on a flask.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Saint George's half-cheek in a brooch.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Ay, and in a brooch of lead.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
Ay, and worn in the cap of a tooth-drawer.
And now forward; for we have put thee in countenance.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: You have put me out of countenance.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: False; we have given thee faces.

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: But you have out-faced them all.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: An thou wert a lion, we would do so.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text:
Therefore, as he is an ass, let him go.
And so adieu, sweet Jude! nay, why dost thou stay?

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: For the latter end of his name.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: For the ass to the Jude; give it him:—Jud-as, away!

units.speaker: HOLOFERNES
units.text: This is not generous, not gentle, not humble.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text:
A light for Monsieur Judas! it grows dark, he may
stumble.
HOLOFERNES retires

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Alas, poor Maccabaeus, how hath he been baited!
Enter DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO, for Hector

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Hide thy head, Achilles: here comes Hector in arms.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Though my mocks come home by me, I will now be merry.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Hector was but a Troyan in respect of this.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: But is this Hector?

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: I think Hector was not so clean-timbered.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text: His leg is too big for Hector's.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: More calf, certain.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: No; he is best endued in the small.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: This cannot be Hector.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: He's a god or a painter; for he makes faces.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
The armipotent Mars, of lances the almighty,
Gave Hector a gift,—

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: A gilt nutmeg.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: A lemon.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text: Stuck with cloves.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: No, cloven.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
Peace!—
The armipotent Mars, of lances the almighty
Gave Hector a gift, the heir of Ilion;
A man so breathed, that certain he would fight; yea
From morn till night, out of his pavilion.
I am that flower,—

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: That mint.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text: That columbine.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text: Sweet Lord Longaville, rein thy tongue.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text:
I must rather give it the rein, for it runs against
Hector.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Ay, and Hector's a greyhound.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
The sweet war-man is dead and rotten; sweet chucks,
beat not the bones of the buried: when he breathed,
he was a man. But I will forward with my device.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text: Sweet royalty, bestow on me the sense of hearing.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Speak, brave Hector: we are much delighted.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text: I do adore thy sweet grace's slipper.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: [Aside to DUMAIN] Loves her by the foot,—

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: [Aside to BOYET] He may not by the yard.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text: This Hector far surmounted Hannibal,—

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
The party is gone, fellow Hector, she is gone; she
is two months on her way.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text: What meanest thou?

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
Faith, unless you play the honest Troyan, the poor
wench is cast away: she's quick; the child brags in
her belly already: tis yours.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
Dost thou infamonize me among potentates? thou shalt
die.

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
Then shall Hector be whipped for Jaquenetta that is
quick by him and hanged for Pompey that is dead by
him.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Most rare Pompey!

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text: Renowned Pompey!

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
Greater than great, great, great, great Pompey!
Pompey the Huge!

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Hector trembles.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
Pompey is moved. More Ates, more Ates! stir them
on! stir them on!

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Hector will challenge him.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
Ay, if a' have no man's blood in's belly than will
sup a flea.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text: By the north pole, I do challenge thee.

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text:
I will not fight with a pole, like a northern man:
I'll slash; I'll do it by the sword. I bepray you,
let me borrow my arms again.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Room for the incensed Worthies!

units.speaker: COSTARD
units.text: I'll do it in my shirt.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Most resolute Pompey!

units.speaker: MOTH
units.text:
Master, let me take you a buttonhole lower. Do you
not see Pompey is uncasing for the combat? What mean
you? You will lose your reputation.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
Gentlemen and soldiers, pardon me; I will not combat
in my shirt.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: You may not deny it: Pompey hath made the challenge.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text: Sweet bloods, I both may and will.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: What reason have you for't?

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
The naked truth of it is, I have no shirt; I go
woolward for penance.

units.speaker: BOYET
units.text:
True, and it was enjoined him in Rome for want of
linen: since when, I'll be sworn, he wore none but
a dishclout of Jaquenetta's, and that a' wears next
his heart for a favour.
Enter MERCADE

units.speaker: MERCADE
units.text: God save you, madam!

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
Welcome, Mercade;
But that thou interrupt'st our merriment.

units.speaker: MERCADE
units.text:
I am sorry, madam; for the news I bring
Is heavy in my tongue. The king your father—

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Dead, for my life!

units.speaker: MERCADE
units.text: Even so; my tale is told.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: Worthies, away! the scene begins to cloud.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
For mine own part, I breathe free breath. I have
seen the day of wrong through the little hole of
discretion, and I will right myself like a soldier.
Exeunt Worthies

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: How fares your majesty?

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Boyet, prepare; I will away tonight.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Madam, not so; I do beseech you, stay.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
Prepare, I say. I thank you, gracious lords,
For all your fair endeavors; and entreat,
Out of a new-sad soul, that you vouchsafe
In your rich wisdom to excuse or hide
The liberal opposition of our spirits,
If over-boldly we have borne ourselves
In the converse of breath: your gentleness
Was guilty of it. Farewell worthy lord!
A heavy heart bears not a nimble tongue:
Excuse me so, coming too short of thanks
For my great suit so easily obtain'd.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
The extreme parts of time extremely forms
All causes to the purpose of his speed,
And often at his very loose decides
That which long process could not arbitrate:
And though the mourning brow of progeny
Forbid the smiling courtesy of love
The holy suit which fain it would convince,
Yet, since love's argument was first on foot,
Let not the cloud of sorrow justle it
From what it purposed; since, to wail friends lost
Is not by much so wholesome-profitable
As to rejoice at friends but newly found.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: I understand you not: my griefs are double.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
Honest plain words best pierce the ear of grief;
And by these badges understand the king.
For your fair sakes have we neglected time,
Play'd foul play with our oaths: your beauty, ladies,
Hath much deform'd us, fashioning our humours
Even to the opposed end of our intents:
And what in us hath seem'd ridiculous,—
As love is full of unbefitting strains,
All wanton as a child, skipping and vain,
Form'd by the eye and therefore, like the eye,
Full of strange shapes, of habits and of forms,
Varying in subjects as the eye doth roll
To every varied object in his glance:
Which parti-coated presence of loose love
Put on by us, if, in your heavenly eyes,
Have misbecomed our oaths and gravities,
Those heavenly eyes, that look into these faults,
Suggested us to make. Therefore, ladies,
Our love being yours, the error that love makes
Is likewise yours: we to ourselves prove false,
By being once false for ever to be true
To those that make us both,—fair ladies, you:
And even that falsehood, in itself a sin,
Thus purifies itself and turns to grace.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
We have received your letters full of love;
Your favours, the ambassadors of love;
And, in our maiden council, rated them
At courtship, pleasant jest and courtesy,
As bombast and as lining to the time:
But more devout than this in our respects
Have we not been; and therefore met your loves
In their own fashion, like a merriment.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: Our letters, madam, show'd much more than jest.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text: So did our looks.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text: We did not quote them so.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
Now, at the latest minute of the hour,
Grant us your loves.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
A time, methinks, too short
To make a world-without-end bargain in.
No, no, my lord, your grace is perjured much,
Full of dear guiltiness; and therefore this:
If for my love, as there is no such cause,
You will do aught, this shall you do for me:
Your oath I will not trust; but go with speed
To some forlorn and naked hermitage,
Remote from all the pleasures of the world;
There stay until the twelve celestial signs
Have brought about the annual reckoning.
If this austere insociable life
Change not your offer made in heat of blood;
If frosts and fasts, hard lodging and thin weeds
Nip not the gaudy blossoms of your love,
But that it bear this trial and last love;
Then, at the expiration of the year,
Come challenge me, challenge me by these deserts,
And, by this virgin palm now kissing thine
I will be thine; and till that instant shut
My woeful self up in a mourning house,
Raining the tears of lamentation
For the remembrance of my father's death.
If this thou do deny, let our hands part,
Neither entitled in the other's heart.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text:
If this, or more than this, I would deny,
To flatter up these powers of mine with rest,
The sudden hand of death close up mine eye!
Hence ever then my heart is in thy breast.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: But what to me, my love? but what to me? A wife?

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text:
A beard, fair health, and honesty;
With three-fold love I wish you all these three.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: O, shall I say, I thank you, gentle wife?

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text:
Not so, my lord; a twelvemonth and a day
I'll mark no words that smooth-faced wooers say:
Come when the king doth to my lady come;
Then, if I have much love, I'll give you some.

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: I'll serve thee true and faithfully till then.

units.speaker: KATHARINE
units.text: Yet swear not, lest ye be forsworn again.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text: What says Maria?

units.speaker: MARIA
units.text:
At the twelvemonth's end
I'll change my black gown for a faithful friend.

units.speaker: LONGAVILLE
units.text: I'll stay with patience; but the time is long.

units.speaker: MARIA
units.text: The liker you; few taller are so young.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
Studies my lady? mistress, look on me;
Behold the window of my heart, mine eye,
What humble suit attends thy answer there:
Impose some service on me for thy love.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
Oft have I heard of you, my Lord Biron,
Before I saw you; and the world's large tongue
Proclaims you for a man replete with mocks,
Full of comparisons and wounding flouts,
Which you on all estates will execute
That lie within the mercy of your wit.
To weed this wormwood from your fruitful brain,
And therewithal to win me, if you please,
Without the which I am not to be won,
You shall this twelvemonth term from day to day
Visit the speechless sick and still converse
With groaning wretches; and your task shall be,
With all the fierce endeavor of your wit
To enforce the pained impotent to smile.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
To move wild laughter in the throat of death?
It cannot be; it is impossible:
Mirth cannot move a soul in agony.

units.speaker: ROSALINE
units.text:
Why, that's the way to choke a gibing spirit,
Whose influence is begot of that loose grace
Which shallow laughing hearers give to fools:
A jest's prosperity lies in the ear
Of him that hears it, never in the tongue
Of him that makes it: then, if sickly ears,
Deaf'd with the clamours of their own dear groans,
Will hear your idle scorns, continue then,
And I will have you and that fault withal;
But if they will not, throw away that spirit,
And I shall find you empty of that fault,
Right joyful of your reformation.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text:
A twelvemonth! well; befall what will befall,
I'll jest a twelvemonth in an hospital.

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text:
[To FERDINAND] Ay, sweet my lord; and so I take my
leave.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: No, madam; we will bring you on your way.

units.speaker: BIRON
FERDINAND
units.text:
Come, sir, it wants a twelvemonth and a day,
And then 'twill end.

units.speaker: BIRON
units.text: That's too long for a play.
Re-enter DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text: Sweet majesty, vouchsafe me,—

units.speaker: PRINCESS
units.text: Was not that Hector?

units.speaker: DUMAIN
units.text: The worthy knight of Troy.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
I will kiss thy royal finger, and take leave. I am
a votary; I have vowed to Jaquenetta to hold the
plough for her sweet love three years. But, most
esteemed greatness, will you hear the dialogue that
the two learned men have compiled in praise of the
owl and the cuckoo? It should have followed in the
end of our show.

units.speaker: FERDINAND
units.text: Call them forth quickly; we will do so.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text: Holla! approach.

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
THE SONG
units.text: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
Spring.
When there are colorful daisies and blue violets
And silver-white cuckoo flowers
And yellow cuckoo-buds
Covering the meadows so wonderfully,
The cuckoo then, on every tree,
Mocks married men, for he sings "Cuckoo!"
"Cuckoo, cuckoo!" Oh what a terrifying word,
That scares the ears of married people!

units.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
units.text:
The words of Mercury are harsh after the songs of
Apollo. You that way: we this way.
Exeunt

sentences.text: Enter FERDINAND king of Navarre, BIRON, LONGAVILLE and DUMAIN

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
Let fame, that all hunt after in their lives,
Live register'd upon our brazen tombs
And then grace us in the disgrace of death;
When, spite of cormorant devouring Time,
The endeavor of this present breath may buy
That honour which shall bate his scythe's keen edge
And make us heirs of all eternity.
Therefore, brave conquerors,—for so you are,
That war against your own affections
And the huge army of the world's desires,—
Our late edict shall strongly stand in force:
Navarre shall be the wonder of the world;
Our court shall be a little Academe,
Still and contemplative in living art.
You three, Biron, Dumain, and Longaville,
Have sworn for three years' term to live with me
My fellow-scholars, and to keep those statutes
That are recorded in this schedule here:
Your oaths are pass'd; and now subscribe your names,
That his own hand may strike his honour down
That violates the smallest branch herein:
If you are arm'd to do as sworn to do,
Subscribe to your deep oaths, and keep it too.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text:
I am resolved; 'tis but a three years' fast:
The mind shall banquet, though the body pine:
Fat paunches have lean pates, and dainty bits
Make rich the ribs, but bankrupt quite the wits.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text:
My loving lord, Dumain is mortified:
The grosser manner of these world's delights
He throws upon the gross world's baser slaves:
To love, to wealth, to pomp, I pine and die;
With all these living in philosophy.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
I can but say their protestation over;
So much, dear liege, I have already sworn,
That is, to live and study here three years.
But there are other strict observances;
As, not to see a woman in that term,
Which I hope well is not enrolled there;
And one day in a week to touch no food
And but one meal on every day beside,
The which I hope is not enrolled there;
And then, to sleep but three hours in the night,
And not be seen to wink of all the day—
When I was wont to think no harm all night
And make a dark night too of half the day—
Which I hope well is not enrolled there:
O, these are barren tasks, too hard to keep,
Not to see ladies, study, fast, not sleep!

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Your oath is pass'd to pass away from these.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Let me say no, my liege, an if you please:
I only swore to study with your grace
And stay here in your court for three years' space.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text: You swore to that, Biron, and to the rest.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
By yea and nay, sir, then I swore in jest.
What is the end of study? let me know.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
By yea and nay, sir, then I swore in jest.
What is the end of study? let me know.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Why, that to know, which else we should not know.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Things hid and barr'd, you mean, from common sense?

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Ay, that is study's godlike recompense.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Come on, then; I will swear to study so,
To know the thing I am forbid to know:
As thus,—to study where I well may dine,
When I to feast expressly am forbid;
Or study where to meet some mistress fine,
When mistresses from common sense are hid;
Or, having sworn too hard a keeping oath,
Study to break it and not break my troth.
If study's gain be thus and this be so,
Study knows that which yet it doth not know:
Swear me to this, and I will ne'er say no.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
These be the stops that hinder study quite
And train our intellects to vain delight.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Why, all delights are vain; but that most vain,
Which with pain purchased doth inherit pain:
As, painfully to pore upon a book
To seek the light of truth; while truth the while
Doth falsely blind the eyesight of his look:
Light seeking light doth light of light beguile:
So, ere you find where light in darkness lies,
Your light grows dark by losing of your eyes.
Study me how to please the eye indeed
By fixing it upon a fairer eye,
Who dazzling so, that eye shall be his heed
And give him light that it was blinded by.
Study is like the heaven's glorious sun
That will not be deep-search'd with saucy looks:
Small have continual plodders ever won
Save base authority from others' books
These earthly godfathers of heaven's lights
That give a name to every fixed star
Have no more profit of their shining nights
Than those that walk and wot not what they are.
Too much to know is to know nought but fame;
And every godfather can give a name.

Too much to know is to know nought but fame;
And every godfather can give a name.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Why, all delights are vain; but that most vain,
Which with pain purchased doth inherit pain:
As, painfully to pore upon a book
To seek the light of truth; while truth the while
Doth falsely blind the eyesight of his look:
Light seeking light doth light of light beguile:
So, ere you find where light in darkness lies,
Your light grows dark by losing of your eyes.
Study me how to please the eye indeed
By fixing it upon a fairer eye,
Who dazzling so, that eye shall be his heed
And give him light that it was blinded by.
Study is like the heaven's glorious sun
That will not be deep-search'd with saucy looks:
Small have continual plodders ever won
Save base authority from others' books
These earthly godfathers of heaven's lights
That give a name to every fixed star
Have no more profit of their shining nights
Than those that walk and wot not what they are.
Too much to know is to know nought but fame;
And every godfather can give a name.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: How well he's read, to reason against reading!

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: Proceeded well, to stop all good proceeding!

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text: He weeds the corn and still lets grow the weeding.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: The spring is near when green geese are a-breeding.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: How follows that?

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Fit in his place and time.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: In reason nothing.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Something then in rhyme.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
Biron is like an envious sneaping frost,
That bites the first-born infants of the spring.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Well, say I am; why should proud summer boast
Before the birds have any cause to sing?
Why should I joy in any abortive birth?
At Christmas I no more desire a rose
Than wish a snow in May's new-fangled mirth;
But like of each thing that in season grows.
So you, to study now it is too late,
Climb o'er the house to unlock the little gate.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Well, sit you out: go home, Biron: adieu.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Well, sit you out: go home, Biron: adieu.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
No, my good lord; I have sworn to stay with you:
And though I have for barbarism spoke more
Than for that angel knowledge you can say,
Yet confident I'll keep what I have swore
And bide the penance of each three years' day.
Give me the paper; let me read the same;
And to the strict'st decrees I'll write my name.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: How well this yielding rescues thee from shame!

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
[Reads] 'Item, That no woman shall come within a
mile of my court:' Hath this been proclaimed?

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text: Four days ago.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Let's see the penalty.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Let's see the penalty.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
'On pain of losing her tongue.' Who devised this
penalty?

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text: Marry, that did I.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Sweet lord, and why?

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text: To fright them hence with that dread penalty.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: A dangerous law against gentility!

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: A dangerous law against gentility!

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
'Item, If any man be seen to talk with a woman
within the term of three years, he shall endure such
public shame as the rest of the court can possibly
devise.'
This article, my liege, yourself must break;
For well you know here comes in embassy
The French King's daughter with yourself to speak—
A maid of grace and complete majesty—
About surrender up of Aquitaine
To her decrepit, sick and bedrid father:
Therefore this article is made in vain,
Or vainly comes the admired Princess hither.

sentences.text: What say you, lords? Why, this was quite forgot.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: What say you, lords? Why, this was quite forgot.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
So study evermore is overshot:
While it doth study to have what it would
It doth forget to do the thing it should,
And when it hath the thing it hunteth most,
'Tis won as towns with fire, so won, so lost.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
We must of force dispense with this decree;
She must lie here on mere necessity.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Necessity will make us all forsworn
Three thousand times within this three years' space;
For every man with his affects is born,
Not by might master'd but by special grace:
If I break faith, this word shall speak for me;
I am forsworn on 'mere necessity.'
So to the laws at large I write my name:

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
And he that breaks them in the least degree
Stands in attainder of eternal shame:
Suggestions are to other as to me;
But I believe, although I seem so loath,
I am the last that will last keep his oath.
But is there no quick recreation granted?

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
Ay, that there is. Our court, you know, is haunted
With a refined traveller of Spain;
A man in all the world's new fashion planted,
That hath a mint of phrases in his brain;
One whom the music of his own vain tongue
Doth ravish like enchanting harmony;
A man of complements, whom right and wrong
Have chose as umpire of their mutiny:
This child of fancy, that Armado hight,
For interim to our studies shall relate
In high-born words the worth of many a knight
From tawny Spain lost in the world's debate.
How you delight, my lords, I know not, I;
But, I protest, I love to hear him lie
And I will use him for my minstrelsy .
