sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
Fauste, precor gelida quando pecus omne sub umbra
Ruminat,—and so forth. Ah, good old Mantuan! I
may speak of thee as the traveller doth of Venice;
Venetia, Venetia,
Chi non ti vede non ti pretia.
Old Mantuan, old Mantuan! who understandeth thee
not, loves thee not. Ut, re, sol, la, mi, fa.
Under pardon, sir, what are the contents? or rather,
as Horace says in his—What, my soul, verses?

sentences.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
sentences.text: Ay, sir, and very learned.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: Let me hear a staff, a stanze, a verse; lege, domine.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: Let me hear a staff, a stanze, a verse; lege, domine.

sentences.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
sentences.text:
[Reads]
If love make me forsworn, how shall I swear to love?
Ah, never faith could hold, if not to beauty vow'd!
Though to myself forsworn, to thee I'll faithful prove:
Those thoughts to me were oaks, to thee like
osiers bow'd.
Study his bias leaves and makes his book thine eyes,
Where all those pleasures live that art would
comprehend:
If knowledge be the mark, to know thee shall suffice;
Well learned is that tongue that well can thee commend,
All ignorant that soul that sees thee without wonder;
Which is to me some praise that I thy parts admire:
Thy eye Jove's lightning bears, thy voice his dreadful
thunder,
Which not to anger bent, is music and sweet fire.
Celestial as thou art, O, pardon, love, this wrong,
That sings heaven's praise with such an earthly tongue.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
You find not the apostraphas, and so miss the
accent: let me supervise the canzonet. Here are
only numbers ratified; but, for the elegancy,
facility, and golden cadence of poesy, caret.
Ovidius Naso was the man: and why, indeed, Naso,
but for smelling out the odouriferous flowers of
fancy, the jerks of invention? Imitari is nothing:
so doth the hound his master, the ape his keeper,
the tired horse his rider. But, damosella virgin,
was this directed to you?

sentences.speaker: JAQUENETTA
sentences.text:
Ay, sir, from one Monsieur Biron, one of the strange
queen's lords.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
I will overglance the superscript: 'To the
snow-white hand of the most beauteous Lady
Rosaline.' I will look again on the intellect of
the letter, for the nomination of the party writing
to the person written unto: 'Your ladyship's in all
desired employment, BIRON.' Sir Nathaniel, this
Biron is one of the votaries with the king; and here
he hath framed a letter to a sequent of the stranger
queen's, which accidentally, or by the way of
progression, hath miscarried. Trip and go, my
sweet; deliver this paper into the royal hand of the
king: it may concern much. Stay not thy
compliment; I forgive thy duty; adieu.

sentences.speaker: JAQUENETTA
sentences.text: Good Costard, go with me. Sir, God save your life!

sentences.speaker: JAQUENETTA
sentences.text: Good Costard, go with me. Sir, God save your life!

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text: Have with thee, my girl.
Exeunt COSTARD and JAQUENETTA

sentences.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
sentences.text:
Sir, you have done this in the fear of God, very
religiously; and, as a certain father saith,—

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
Sir tell me not of the father; I do fear colourable
colours. But to return to the verses: did they
please you, Sir Nathaniel?

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
Sir tell me not of the father; I do fear colourable
colours. But to return to the verses: did they
please you, Sir Nathaniel?

sentences.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
sentences.text: Marvellous well for the pen.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
I do dine to-day at the father's of a certain pupil
of mine; where, if, before repast, it shall please
you to gratify the table with a grace, I will, on my
privilege I have with the parents of the foresaid
child or pupil, undertake your ben venuto; where I
will prove those verses to be very unlearned,
neither savouring of poetry, wit, nor invention: I
beseech your society.

sentences.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL

And thank you too; for society, saith the text, is
the happiness of life.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: And, certes, the text most infallibly concludes it.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
Sir, I do invite you too; you shall not
say me nay: pauca verba. Away! the gentles are at
their game, and we will to our recreation.

Enter BIRON, with a paper

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
The king he is hunting the deer; I am coursing
myself: they have pitched a toil; I am toiling in
a pitch,—pitch that defiles: defile! a foul
word. Well, set thee down, sorrow! for so they say
the fool said, and so say I, and I the fool: well
proved, wit! By the Lord, this love is as mad as
Ajax: it kills sheep; it kills me, I a sheep:
well proved again o' my side! I will not love: if
I do, hang me; i' faith, I will not. O, but her
eye,—by this light, but for her eye, I would not
love her; yes, for her two eyes. Well, I do nothing
in the world but lie, and lie in my throat. By
heaven, I do love: and it hath taught me to rhyme
and to be melancholy; and here is part of my rhyme,
and here my melancholy. Well, she hath one o' my
sonnets already: the clown bore it, the fool sent
it, and the lady hath it: sweet clown, sweeter
fool, sweetest lady! By the world, I would not care
a pin, if the other three were in. Here comes one
with a paper: God give him grace to groan!

Enter FERDINAND, with a paper

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Ay me!

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
[Aside] Shot, by heaven! Proceed, sweet Cupid:
thou hast thumped him with thy bird-bolt under the
left pap. In faith, secrets!

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
[Aside] Shot, by heaven! Proceed, sweet Cupid:
thou hast thumped him with thy bird-bolt under the
left pap. In faith, secrets!

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
[Aside] Shot, by heaven! Proceed, sweet Cupid:
thou hast thumped him with thy bird-bolt under the
left pap. In faith, secrets!

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
[Reads]
So sweet a kiss the golden sun gives not
To those fresh morning drops upon the rose,
As thy eye-beams, when their fresh rays have smote
The night of dew that on my cheeks down flows:
Nor shines the silver moon one half so bright
Through the transparent bosom of the deep,
As doth thy face through tears of mine give light;
Thou shinest in every tear that I do weep:
No drop but as a coach doth carry thee;
So ridest thou triumphing in my woe.
Do but behold the tears that swell in me,
And they thy glory through my grief will show:
But do not love thyself; then thou wilt keep
My tears for glasses, and still make me weep.
O queen of queens! how far dost thou excel,
No thought can think, nor tongue of mortal tell.
How shall she know my griefs? I'll drop the paper:
Sweet leaves, shade folly. Who is he comes here?

So sweet a kiss the golden sun gives not
To those fresh morning drops upon the rose,
As thy eye-beams, when their fresh rays have smote
The night of dew that on my cheeks down flows:
Nor shines the silver moon one half so bright
Through the transparent bosom of the deep,
As doth thy face through tears of mine give light;
Thou shinest in every tear that I do weep:
No drop but as a coach doth carry thee;
So ridest thou triumphing in my woe.
Do but behold the tears that swell in me,
And they thy glory through my grief will show:
But do not love thyself; then thou wilt keep
My tears for glasses, and still make me weep.
O queen of queens! how far dost thou excel,
No thought can think, nor tongue of mortal tell.
How shall she know my griefs? I'll drop the paper:
Sweet leaves, shade folly. Who is he comes here?

O sweet Maria, empress of my love!
These numbers will I tear, and write in prose.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text:
Did not the heavenly rhetoric of thine eye,
'Gainst whom the world cannot hold argument,
Persuade my heart to this false perjury?
Vows for thee broke deserve not punishment.
A woman I forswore; but I will prove,
Thou being a goddess, I forswore not thee:
My vow was earthly, thou a heavenly love;
Thy grace being gain'd cures all disgrace in me.
Vows are but breath, and breath a vapour is:
Then thou, fair sun, which on my earth dost shine,
Exhalest this vapour-vow; in thee it is:
If broken then, it is no fault of mine:
If by me broke, what fool is not so wise
To lose an oath to win a paradise?

A green goose a goddess: pure, pure idolatry.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text:
[Reads]
On a day—alack the day!—
Love, whose month is ever May,
Spied a blossom passing fair
Playing in the wanton air:
Through the velvet leaves the wind,
All unseen, can passage find;
That the lover, sick to death,
Wish himself the heaven's breath.
Air, quoth he, thy cheeks may blow;
Air, would I might triumph so!
But, alack, my hand is sworn
Ne'er to pluck thee from thy thorn;
Vow, alack, for youth unmeet,
Youth so apt to pluck a sweet!
Do not call it sin in me,
That I am forsworn for thee;
Thou for whom Jove would swear
Juno but an Ethiope were;
And deny himself for Jove,
Turning mortal for thy love.
This will I send, and something else more plain,
That shall express my true love's fasting pain.
O, would the king, Biron, and Longaville,
Were lovers too! Ill, to example ill,
Would from my forehead wipe a perjured note;
For none offend where all alike do dote.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text:
[Advancing] Dumain, thy love is far from charity.
That in love's grief desirest society.
You may look pale, but I should blush, I know,
To be o'erheard and taken napping so.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text:
[Advancing] Dumain, thy love is far from charity.
That in love's grief desirest society.
You may look pale, but I should blush, I know,
To be o'erheard and taken napping so.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
[Advancing] Come, sir, you blush; as his your case is
such;
You chide at him, offending twice as much;
You do not love Maria? Longaville
Did never sonnet for her sake compile,
Nor never lay his wreathed arms athwart
His loving bosom to keep down his heart.
I have been closely shrouded in this bush
And mark'd you both and for you both did blush:
I heard your guilty rhymes, observed your fashion,
Saw sighs reek from you, noted well your passion:
Ay me! says one; O Jove! the other cries;
One, her hairs were gold, crystal the other's eyes:

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
[Advancing] Come, sir, you blush; as his your case is
such;
You chide at him, offending twice as much;
You do not love Maria? Longaville
Did never sonnet for her sake compile,
Nor never lay his wreathed arms athwart
His loving bosom to keep down his heart.
I have been closely shrouded in this bush
And mark'd you both and for you both did blush:
I heard your guilty rhymes, observed your fashion,
Saw sighs reek from you, noted well your passion:
Ay me! says one; O Jove! the other cries;
One, her hairs were gold, crystal the other's eyes:

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: You would for paradise break faith, and troth;

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: You would for paradise break faith, and troth;

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
And Jove, for your love, would infringe an oath.
What will Biron say when that he shall hear
Faith so infringed, which such zeal did swear?
How will he scorn! how will he spend his wit!
How will he triumph, leap and laugh at it!
For all the wealth that ever I did see,
I would not have him know so much by me.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Now step I forth to whip hypocrisy.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Now step I forth to whip hypocrisy.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Ah, good my liege, I pray thee, pardon me!
Good heart, what grace hast thou, thus to reprove
These worms for loving, that art most in love?
Your eyes do make no coaches; in your tears
There is no certain princess that appears;
You'll not be perjured, 'tis a hateful thing;
Tush, none but minstrels like of sonneting!
But are you not ashamed? nay, are you not,
All three of you, to be thus much o'ershot?
You found his mote; the king your mote did see;
But I a beam do find in each of three.
O, what a scene of foolery have I seen,
Of sighs, of groans, of sorrow and of teen!
O me, with what strict patience have I sat,
To see a king transformed to a gnat!
To see great Hercules whipping a gig,
And profound Solomon to tune a jig,
And Nestor play at push-pin with the boys,
And critic Timon laugh at idle toys!
Where lies thy grief, O, tell me, good Dumain?
And gentle Longaville, where lies thy pain?
And where my liege's? all about the breast:
A caudle, ho!

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
Too bitter is thy jest.
Are we betray'd thus to thy over-view?

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
Too bitter is thy jest.
Are we betray'd thus to thy over-view?

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Not you to me, but I betray'd by you:
I, that am honest; I, that hold it sin
To break the vow I am engaged in;
I am betray'd, by keeping company
With men like men of inconstancy.
When shall you see me write a thing in rhyme?
Or groan for love? or spend a minute's time
In pruning me? When shall you hear that I
Will praise a hand, a foot, a face, an eye,
A gait, a state, a brow, a breast, a waist,
A leg, a limb?

sentences.text:
Soft! whither away so fast?
A true man or a thief that gallops so?

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
Soft! whither away so fast?
A true man or a thief that gallops so?

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: I post from love: good lover, let me go.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: I post from love: good lover, let me go.
Enter JAQUENETTA and COSTARD

sentences.speaker: JAQUENETTA
sentences.text: God bless the King!

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: What present hast thou there?

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text: Some certain treason.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: What makes treason here?

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text: Nay, it makes nothing, sir.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
If it mar nothing neither,
The treason and you go in peace away together.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
If it mar nothing neither,
The treason and you go in peace away together.

sentences.speaker: JAQUENETTA
sentences.text:
I beseech your grace, let this letter be read:
Our parson misdoubts it; 'twas treason, he said.

sentences.speaker: JAQUENETTA
sentences.text:
I beseech your grace, let this letter be read:
Our parson misdoubts it; 'twas treason, he said.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Biron, read it over.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Biron, read it over.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Where hadst thou it?

sentences.speaker: JAQUENETTA
sentences.text: Of Costard.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Where hadst thou it?

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text: Of Dun Adramadio, Dun Adramadio.

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text: Of Dun Adramadio, Dun Adramadio.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: How now! what is in you? why dost thou tear it?

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: How now! what is in you? why dost thou tear it?

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: How now! what is in you? why dost thou tear it?

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: A toy, my liege, a toy: your grace needs not fear it.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text:
It did move him to passion, and therefore let's hear
it.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: It is Biron's writing, and here is his name.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: It is Biron's writing, and here is his name.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
[To COSTARD] Ah, you whoreson loggerhead! you were
born to do me shame.
Guilty, my lord, guilty! I confess, I confess.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: What?

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
That you three fools lack'd me fool to make up the
mess:
He, he, and you, and you, my liege, and I,
Are pick-purses in love, and we deserve to die.
O, dismiss this audience, and I shall tell you more.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: Now the number is even.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
True, true; we are four.
Will these turtles be gone?

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
True, true; we are four.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Hence, sirs; away!

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text: Walk aside the true folk, and let the traitors stay.
Exeunt COSTARD and JAQUENETTA

sentences.speaker: BIRON
BIRON
BIRON
FERDINAND
sentences.text: What, did these rent lines show some love of thine?

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Did they, quoth you? Who sees the heavenly Rosaline,
That, like a rude and savage man of Inde,
At the first opening of the gorgeous east,
Bows not his vassal head and strucken blind
Kisses the base ground with obedient breast?
What peremptory eagle-sighted eye
Dares look upon the heaven of her brow,
That is not blinded by her majesty?

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
FERDINAND
BIRON
sentences.text:
My eyes are then no eyes, nor I Biron:
O, but for my love, day would turn to night!
Of all complexions the cull'd sovereignty
Do meet, as at a fair, in her fair cheek,
Where several worthies make one dignity,
Where nothing wants that want itself doth seek.
Lend me the flourish of all gentle tongues,—
Fie, painted rhetoric! O, she needs it not:
To things of sale a seller's praise belongs,
She passes praise; then praise too short doth blot.
A wither'd hermit, five-score winters worn,
Might shake off fifty, looking in her eye:
Beauty doth varnish age, as if new-born,
And gives the crutch the cradle's infancy:
O, 'tis the sun that maketh all things shine.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: By heaven, thy love is black as ebony.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Is ebony like her? O wood divine!
A wife of such wood were felicity.
O, who can give an oath? where is a book?
That I may swear beauty doth beauty lack,
If that she learn not of her eye to look:
No face is fair that is not full so black.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
FERDINAND
FERDINAND
BIRON
sentences.text:
Devils soonest tempt, resembling spirits of light.
O, if in black my lady's brows be deck'd,
It mourns that painting and usurping hair
Should ravish doters with a false aspect;
And therefore is she born to make black fair.
Her favour turns the fashion of the days,
For native blood is counted painting now;
And therefore red, that would avoid dispraise,
Paints itself black, to imitate her brow.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: To look like her are chimney-sweepers black.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text: And since her time are colliers counted bright.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: And Ethiopes of their sweet complexion crack.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: Dark needs no candles now, for dark is light.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Your mistresses dare never come in rain,
For fear their colours should be wash'd away.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
'Twere good, yours did; for, sir, to tell you plain,
I'll find a fairer face not wash'd to-day.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: I'll prove her fair, or talk till doomsday here.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: No devil will fright thee then so much as she.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: I never knew man hold vile stuff so dear.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text: Look, here's thy love: my foot and her face see.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
O, if the streets were paved with thine eyes,
Her feet were much too dainty for such tread!

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text:
O, vile! then, as she goes, what upward lies
The street should see as she walk'd overhead.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text:
O, vile! then, as she goes, what upward lies
The street should see as she walk'd overhead.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: But what of this? are we not all in love?

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: But what of this? are we not all in love?

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Nothing so sure; and thereby all forsworn.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
Then leave this chat; and, good Biron, now prove
Our loving lawful, and our faith not torn.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: Ay, marry, there; some flattery for this evil.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text:
O, some authority how to proceed;
Some tricks, some quillets, how to cheat the devil.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: Some salve for perjury.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
'Tis more than need.
Have at you, then, affection's men at arms.
Consider what you first did swear unto,
To fast, to study, and to see no woman;
Flat treason 'gainst the kingly state of youth.
Say, can you fast? your stomachs are too young;
And abstinence engenders maladies.
O, we have made a vow to study, lords,
And in that vow we have forsworn our books.
For when would you, my liege, or you, or you,
In leaden contemplation have found out
Such fiery numbers as the prompting eyes
Of beauty's tutors have enrich'd you with?
Other slow arts entirely keep the brain;
And therefore, finding barren practisers,
Scarce show a harvest of their heavy toil:
But love, first learned in a lady's eyes,
Lives not alone immured in the brain;
But, with the motion of all elements,
Courses as swift as thought in every power,
And gives to every power a double power,
Above their functions and their offices.
It adds a precious seeing to the eye;
A lover's eyes will gaze an eagle blind;
A lover's ear will hear the lowest sound,
When the suspicious head of theft is stopp'd:
Love's feeling is more soft and sensible
Than are the tender horns of cockl'd snails;
Love's tongue proves dainty Bacchus gross in taste:
For valour, is not Love a Hercules,
Still climbing trees in the Hesperides?
Subtle as Sphinx; as sweet and musical
As bright Apollo's lute, strung with his hair:
And when Love speaks, the voice of all the gods
Makes heaven drowsy with the harmony.
Never durst poet touch a pen to write
Until his ink were temper'd with Love's sighs;
O, then his lines would ravish savage ears
And plant in tyrants mild humility.
From women's eyes this doctrine I derive:
They sparkle still the right Promethean fire;
They are the books, the arts, the academes,
That show, contain and nourish all the world:
Else none at all in ought proves excellent.
Then fools you were these women to forswear,
Or keeping what is sworn, you will prove fools.
For wisdom's sake, a word that all men love,
Or for love's sake, a word that loves all men,
Or for men's sake, the authors of these women,
Or women's sake, by whom we men are men,
Let us once lose our oaths to find ourselves,
Or else we lose ourselves to keep our oaths.
It is religion to be thus forsworn,
For charity itself fulfills the law,
And who can sever love from charity?

As bright Apollo's lute, strung with his hair:
And when Love speaks, the voice of all the gods
Makes heaven drowsy with the harmony.
Never durst poet touch a pen to write
Until his ink were temper'd with Love's sighs;
O, then his lines would ravish savage ears
And plant in tyrants mild humility.
From women's eyes this doctrine I derive:
They sparkle still the right Promethean fire;
They are the books, the arts, the academes,
That show, contain and nourish all the world:
Else none at all in ought proves excellent.
Then fools you were these women to forswear,
Or keeping what is sworn, you will prove fools.
For wisdom's sake, a word that all men love,
Or for love's sake, a word that loves all men,
Or for men's sake, the authors of these women,
Or women's sake, by whom we men are men,
Let us once lose our oaths to find ourselves,
Or else we lose ourselves to keep our oaths.
It is religion to be thus forsworn,
For charity itself fulfills the law,
And who can sever love from charity?

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
'Tis more than need.
Have at you, then, affection's men at arms.
Consider what you first did swear unto,
To fast, to study, and to see no woman;
Flat treason 'gainst the kingly state of youth.
Say, can you fast? your stomachs are too young;
And abstinence engenders maladies.
O, we have made a vow to study, lords,
And in that vow we have forsworn our books.
For when would you, my liege, or you, or you,
In leaden contemplation have found out
Such fiery numbers as the prompting eyes
Of beauty's tutors have enrich'd you with?
Other slow arts entirely keep the brain;
And therefore, finding barren practisers,
Scarce show a harvest of their heavy toil:
But love, first learned in a lady's eyes,
Lives not alone immured in the brain;
But, with the motion of all elements,
Courses as swift as thought in every power,
And gives to every power a double power,
Above their functions and their offices.
It adds a precious seeing to the eye;
A lover's eyes will gaze an eagle blind;
A lover's ear will hear the lowest sound,
When the suspicious head of theft is stopp'd:
Love's feeling is more soft and sensible
Than are the tender horns of cockl'd snails;
Love's tongue proves dainty Bacchus gross in taste:
For valour, is not Love a Hercules,
Still climbing trees in the Hesperides?
Subtle as Sphinx; as sweet and musical
As bright Apollo's lute, strung with his hair:
And when Love speaks, the voice of all the gods
Makes heaven drowsy with the harmony.

BIRON

LONGAVILLE
sentences.text:
Now to plain-dealing; lay these glozes by:
Shall we resolve to woo these girls of France?

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text:
Now to plain-dealing; lay these glozes by:
Shall we resolve to woo these girls of France?

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
And win them too: therefore let us devise
Some entertainment for them in their tents.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
And win them too: therefore let us devise
Some entertainment for them in their tents.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
First, from the park let us conduct them thither;
Then homeward every man attach the hand
Of his fair mistress: in the afternoon
We will with some strange pastime solace them,
Such as the shortness of the time can shape;
For revels, dances, masks and merry hours
Forerun fair Love, strewing her way with flowers.

That will betime, and may by us be fitted.

sentences.text: Exeunt
Enter HOLOFERNES, SIR NATHANIEL, and DULL

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: Satis quod sufficit.

sentences.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
sentences.text:
I praise God for you, sir: your reasons at dinner
have been sharp and sententious; pleasant without
scurrility, witty without affection, audacious without
impudency, learned without opinion, and strange with-
out heresy. I did converse this quondam day with
a companion of the King's, who is intituled, nomi-
nated, or called, Don Adriano de Armado.

sentences.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
sentences.text:
I praise God for you, sir: your reasons at dinner
have been sharp and sententious; pleasant without
scurrility, witty without affection, audacious without
impudency, learned without opinion, and strange with-
out heresy. I did converse this quondam day with
a companion of the King's, who is intituled, nomi-
nated, or called, Don Adriano de Armado.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
Novi hominem tanquam te: his humour is lofty, his
discourse peremptory, his tongue filed, his eye
ambitious, his gait majestical, and his general
behavior vain, ridiculous, and thrasonical. He is
too picked, too spruce, too affected, too odd, as it
were, too peregrinate, as I may call it.

sentences.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
sentences.text: A most singular and choice epithet.

sentences.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
sentences.text: A most singular and choice epithet.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
He draweth out the thread of his verbosity finer
than the staple of his argument. I abhor such
fanatical phantasimes, such insociable and
point-devise companions; such rackers of
orthography, as to speak dout, sine b, when he should
say doubt; det, when he should pronounce debt,—d,
e, b, t, not d, e, t: he clepeth a calf, cauf;
half, hauf; neighbour vocatur nebor; neigh
abbreviated ne. This is abhominable,—which he
would call abbominable: it insinuateth me of
insanie: ne intelligis, domine? to make frantic,
lunatic.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
Bon, bon, for bene! Priscian a little scratch'd,
'twill serve.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: Video, et gaudeo.
Enter DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO, MOTH, and COSTARD

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
sentences.text: Chirrah!

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: Quare chirrah, not sirrah?

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
sentences.text: Men of peace, well encountered.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: Most military sir, salutation.

sentences.speaker: MOTH
sentences.text:
[Aside to COSTARD] They have been at a great feast
of languages, and stolen the scraps.

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
O, they have lived long on the alms-basket of words.
I marvel thy master hath not eaten thee for a word;
for thou art not so long by the head as
honorificabilitudinitatibus: thou art easier
swallowed than a flap-dragon.

Lend me your horn to make one, and I will whip about
your infamy manu cita.—a gig of a cuckold's horn.

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
An I had but one penny in the world, thou shouldst
have it to buy gingerbread: hold, there is the very
remuneration I had of thy master, thou halfpenny
purse of wit, thou pigeon-egg of discretion. O, an
the heavens were so pleased that thou wert but my
bastard, what a joyful father wouldst thou make me!
Go to; thou hast it ad dunghill, at the fingers'
ends, as they say.

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
sentences.text:
Sir, it is the king's most sweet pleasure and
affection to congratulate the Princess at her
pavilion in the posteriors of this day, which the
rude multitude call the afternoon.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
The posterior of the day, most generous sir, is
liable, congruent and measurable for the afternoon:
the word is well culled, chose, sweet and apt, I do
assure you, sir, I do assure.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
The posterior of the day, most generous sir, is
liable, congruent and measurable for the afternoon:
the word is well culled, chose, sweet and apt, I do
assure you, sir, I do assure.

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
sentences.text:
Sir, the king is a noble gentleman, and my familiar,
I do assure ye, very good friend: for what is
inward between us, let it pass. I do beseech thee,
remember thy courtesy; I beseech thee, apparel thy
head: and among other important and most serious
designs, and of great import indeed, too, but let
that pass: for I must tell thee, it will please his
grace, by the world, sometime to lean upon my poor
shoulder, and with his royal finger, thus, dally
with my excrement, with my mustachio; but, sweet
heart, let that pass. By the world, I recount no
fable: some certain special honours it pleaseth his
greatness to impart to Armado, a soldier, a man of
travel, that hath seen the world; but let that pass.
The very all of all is,—but, sweet heart, I do
implore secrecy, —that the king would have me
present the princess, sweet chuck, with some
delightful ostentation, or show, or pageant, or
antique, or firework. Now, understanding that the
curate and your sweet self are good at such
eruptions and sudden breaking out of mirth, as it
were, I have acquainted you withal, to the end to
crave your assistance.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
Sir, you shall present before her the Nine Worthies.
Sir Nathaniel, as concerning some entertainment of
time, some
show in the posterior of this day, to be rendered by
our assistants, at the king's command, and this most
gallant, illustrate, and learned gentleman, before
the princess; I say none so fit as to present the
Nine Worthies.

sentences.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
sentences.text: Where will you find men worthy enough to present them?

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
Joshua, yourself; this gallant gentleman,
Judas Maccabaeus; this swain, because of his great
limb or joint, shall pass Pompey the Great; the
page, Hercules,—

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
sentences.text:
Pardon, sir; error: he is not quantity enough for
that Worthy's thumb: he is not so big as the end of his
club.

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
Shall I have audience? he shall present Hercules in
minority: his enter and exit shall be strangling a
snake; and I will have an apology for that purpose.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: MOTH
sentences.text:
An excellent device! so, if any of the audience
hiss, you may cry 'Well done, Hercules! now thou
crushest the snake!' that is the way to make an
offence gracious, though few have the grace to do it.

sentences.speaker: MOTH

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES

We will have, if this fadge not, an antic. I
beseech you, follow.

Via, goodman Dull! thou hast spoken no word all this
while.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES

Via, goodman Dull! thou hast spoken no word all this
while.

sentences.speaker: DULL
sentences.text: Nor understood none neither, sir.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: Allons! we will employ thee.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: Allons! we will employ thee.

sentences.speaker: DULL
sentences.text:
I'll make one in a dance, or so; or I will play
On the tabour to the Worthies, and let them dance the
hay.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: Most dull, honest Dull! To our sport, away!

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES

Exeunt
Enter the PRINCESS, KATHARINE, ROSALINE, and MARIA

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
Sweet hearts, we shall be rich ere we depart,
If fairings come thus plentifully in:
A lady wall'd about with diamonds!
Look you what I have from the loving king.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS

Look you what I have from the loving king.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: Madame, came nothing else along with that?

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
Nothing but this! yes, as much love in rhyme
As would be cramm'd up in a sheet of paper,
Writ o' both sides the leaf, margent and all,
That he was fain to seal on Cupid's name.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:

Writ o' both sides the leaf, margent and all,
That he was fain to seal on Cupid's name.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
That was the way to make his godhead wax,
For he hath been five thousand years a boy.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: Ay, and a shrewd unhappy gallows too.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
You'll ne'er be friends with him; a' kill'd your
sister.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text:
He made her melancholy, sad, and heavy;
And so she died: had she been light, like you,
Of such a merry, nimble, stirring spirit,
She might ha' been a grandam ere she died:
And so may you; for a light heart lives long.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: What's your dark meaning, mouse, of this light word?

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: A light condition in a beauty dark.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: We need more light to find your meaning out.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text:
You'll mar the light by taking it in snuff;
Therefore I'll darkly end the argument.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: Look what you do, you do it still i' the dark.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: So do not you, for you are a light wench.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: Indeed I weigh not you, and therefore light.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: You weigh me not? O, that's you care not for me.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: You weigh me not? O, that's you care not for me.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: Great reason; for 'past cure is still past care.'

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
Well bandied both; a set of wit well play'd.
But Rosaline, you have a favour too:
Who sent it? and what is it?

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
Well bandied both; a set of wit well play'd.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
Well bandied both; a set of wit well play'd.
But Rosaline, you have a favour too:

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
I would you knew:
An if my face were but as fair as yours,
My favour were as great; be witness this.
Nay, I have verses too, I thank Biron:
The numbers true; and, were the numbering too,
I were the fairest goddess on the ground:
I am compared to twenty thousand fairs.
O, he hath drawn my picture in his letter!

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Any thing like?

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: Much in the letters; nothing in the praise.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Beauteous as ink; a good conclusion.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: Fair as a text B in a copy-book.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
'Ware pencils, ho! let me not die your debtor,
My red dominical, my golden letter:
O, that your face were not so full of O's!

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: A pox of that jest! and I beshrew all shrows.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: A pox of that jest! and I beshrew all shrows.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: But, Katharine, what was sent to you from fair Dumain?

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: Madam, this glove.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Did he not send you twain?

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text:
Yes, madam, and moreover
Some thousand verses of a faithful lover,
A huge translation of hypocrisy,
Vilely compiled, profound simplicity.

sentences.speaker: MARIA
sentences.text:
This and these pearls to me sent Longaville:
The letter is too long by half a mile.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
I think no less. Dost thou not wish in heart
The chain were longer and the letter short?

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
I think no less. Dost thou not wish in heart
The chain were longer and the letter short?

sentences.speaker: MARIA
sentences.text: Ay, or I would these hands might never part.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: We are wise girls to mock our lovers so.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
They are worse fools to purchase mocking so.
That same Biron I'll torture ere I go:
O that I knew he were but in by the week!
How I would make him fawn and beg and seek
And wait the season and observe the times
And spend his prodigal wits in bootless rhymes
And shape his service wholly to my hests
And make him proud to make me proud that jests!
So pair-taunt-like would I o'ersway his state
That he should be my fool and I his fate.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
None are so surely caught, when they are catch'd,
As wit turn'd fool: folly, in wisdom hatch'd,
Hath wisdom's warrant and the help of school
And wit's own grace to grace a learned fool.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
None are so surely caught, when they are catch'd,
As wit turn'd fool: folly, in wisdom hatch'd,
Hath wisdom's warrant and the help of school
And wit's own grace to grace a learned fool.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
The blood of youth burns not with such excess
As gravity's revolt to wantonness.

sentences.speaker: MARIA
sentences.text:
Folly in fools bears not so strong a note
As foolery in the wise, when wit doth dote;
Since all the power thereof it doth apply
To prove, by wit, worth in simplicity.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Here comes Boyet, and mirth is in his face.
Enter BOYET

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: O, I am stabb'd with laughter! Where's her grace?

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: O, I am stabb'd with laughter! Where's her grace?

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Thy news Boyet?

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
Prepare, madam, prepare!
Arm, wenches, arm! encounters mounted are
Against your peace: Love doth approach disguised,
Armed in arguments; you'll be surprised:
Muster your wits; stand in your own defence;
Or hide your heads like cowards, and fly hence.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
Saint Denis to Saint Cupid! What are they
That charge their breath against us? say, scout, say.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
Saint Denis to Saint Cupid! What are they
That charge their breath against us? say, scout, say.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
Saint Denis to Saint Cupid! What are they
That charge their breath against us? say, scout, say.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
Under the cool shade of a sycamore
I thought to close mine eyes some half an hour;
When, lo! to interrupt my purposed rest,
Toward that shade I might behold addrest
The king and his companions: warily
I stole into a neighbour thicket by,
And overheard what you shall overhear,
That, by and by, disguised they will be here.
Their herald is a pretty knavish page,
That well by heart hath conn'd his embassage:
Action and accent did they teach him there;
'Thus must thou speak,' and 'thus thy body bear:'
And ever and anon they made a doubt
Presence majestical would put him out,
'For,' quoth the king, 'an angel shalt thou see;
Yet fear not thou, but speak audaciously.'
The boy replied, 'An angel is not evil;
I should have fear'd her had she been a devil.'
With that, all laugh'd and clapp'd him on the shoulder,
Making the bold wag by their praises bolder:
One rubb'd his elbow thus, and fleer'd and swore
A better speech was never spoke before;
Another, with his finger and his thumb,
Cried, 'Via! we will do't, come what will come;'
The third he caper'd, and cried, 'All goes well;'
The fourth turn'd on the toe, and down he fell.
With that, they all did tumble on the ground,
With such a zealous laughter, so profound,
That in this spleen ridiculous appears,
To cheque their folly, passion's solemn tears.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: But what, but what, come they to visit us?

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
They do, they do: and are apparell'd thus.
Like Muscovites or Russians, as I guess.
Their purpose is to parle, to court and dance;
And every one his love-feat will advance
Unto his several mistress, which they'll know
By favours several which they did bestow.

PRINCESS
PRINCESS
PRINCESS
PRINCESS
PRINCESS
ROSALINE
sentences.text: Come on, then; wear the favours most in sight.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: But in this changing what is your intent?

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
The effect of my intent is to cross theirs:
They do it but in mocking merriment;
And mock for mock is only my intent.
Their several counsels they unbosom shall
To loves mistook, and so be mock'd withal
Upon the next occasion that we meet,
With visages displayed, to talk and greet.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: But shall we dance, if they desire to't?

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
PRINCESS
BOYET
sentences.text:
Why, that contempt will kill the speaker's heart,
And quite divorce his memory from his part.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
Therefore I do it; and I make no doubt
The rest will ne'er come in, if he be out
There's no such sport as sport by sport o'erthrown,
To make theirs ours and ours none but our own:
So shall we stay, mocking intended game,
And they, well mock'd, depart away with shame.

There's no such sport as sport by sport o'erthrown,
To make theirs ours and ours none but our own:
So shall we stay, mocking intended game,
And they, well mock'd, depart away with shame.

sentences.speaker: MOTH
sentences.text: All hail, the richest beauties on the earth!—

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: Beauties no richer than rich taffeta.

sentences.speaker: MOTH
sentences.text: A holy parcel of the fairest dames.

sentences.speaker: MOTH
sentences.text: A holy parcel of the fairest dames.

sentences.speaker: MOTH
sentences.text: That ever turn'd their—backs—to mortal views!

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: [Aside to MOTH] Their eyes, villain, their eyes!

sentences.speaker: MOTH
sentences.text: That ever turn'd their eyes to mortal views!—Out—

sentences.speaker: MOTH
sentences.text: That ever turn'd their eyes to mortal views!—Out—

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: True; out indeed.

sentences.speaker: MOTH
sentences.text:
Out of your favours, heavenly spirits, vouchsafe
Not to behold—

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: [Aside to MOTH] Once to behold, rogue.

sentences.speaker: MOTH
sentences.text:
Once to behold with your sun-beamed eyes,
—with your sun-beamed eyes—

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
They will not answer to that epithet;
You were best call it 'daughter-beamed eyes.'

sentences.speaker: MOTH
sentences.text: They do not mark me, and that brings me out.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Is this your perfectness? be gone, you rogue!

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Is this your perfectness? be gone, you rogue!
Exit MOTH

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
What would these strangers? know their minds, Boyet:
If they do speak our language, 'tis our will:
That some plain man recount their purposes
Know what they would.

sentences.text: What would you with the Princess?

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Nothing but peace and gentle visitation.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: What would they, say they?

sentences.text: Nothing but peace and gentle visitation.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: Why, that they have; and bid them so be gone.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: She says, you have it, and you may be gone.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
Say to her, we have measured many miles
To tread a measure with her on this grass.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
They say, that they have measured many a mile
To tread a measure with you on this grass.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
It is not so. Ask them how many inches
Is in one mile: if they have measured many,
The measure then of one is easily told.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:

The measure then of one is easily told.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE

It is not so. Ask them how many inches
Is in one mile: if they have measured many,
The measure then of one is easily told.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
If to come hither you have measured miles,
And many miles, the princess bids you tell
How many inches doth fill up one mile.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Tell her, we measure them by weary steps.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: She hears herself.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
How many weary steps,
Of many weary miles you have o'ergone,
Are number'd in the travel of one mile?

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
We number nothing that we spend for you:
Our duty is so rich, so infinite,
That we may do it still without accompt.
Vouchsafe to show the sunshine of your face,
That we, like savages, may worship it.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
Blessed are clouds, to do as such clouds do!
Vouchsafe, bright moon, and these thy stars, to shine,
Those clouds removed, upon our watery eyne.

sentences.text:
O vain petitioner! beg a greater matter;
Thou now request'st but moonshine in the water.

Then, in our measure do but vouchsafe one change.
Thou bid'st me beg: this begging is not strange.

sentences.text: Will you not dance? How come you thus estranged?

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Will you not dance? How come you thus estranged?

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: You took the moon at full, but now she's changed.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
Yet still she is the moon, and I the man.
The music plays; vouchsafe some motion to it.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
Yet still she is the moon, and I the man.
The music plays; vouchsafe some motion to it.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: Our ears vouchsafe it.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: But your legs should do it.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
Since you are strangers and come here by chance,
We'll not be nice: take hands. We will not dance.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
Since you are strangers and come here by chance,
We'll not be nice: take hands. We will not dance.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Why take we hands, then?

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
Only to part friends:
Curtsy, sweet hearts; and so the measure ends.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
Only to part friends:
Curtsy, sweet hearts; and so the measure ends.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: More measure of this measure; be not nice.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: We can afford no more at such a price.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Prize you yourselves: what buys your company?

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: Your absence only.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: That can never be.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
Then cannot we be bought: and so, adieu;
Twice to your visor, and half once to you.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: If you deny to dance, let's hold more chat.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: In private, then.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: I am best pleased with that.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: White-handed mistress, one sweet word with thee.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS

sentences.text:
Nay then, two treys, and if you grow so nice,
Metheglin, wort, and malmsey: well run, dice!
There's half-a-dozen sweets.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS

sentences.text: One word in secret.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Let it not be sweet.

sentences.speaker: BIRON

sentences.speaker: MARIA
sentences.text: Name it.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: Fair lady,—

sentences.speaker: MARIA
sentences.text:
Say you so? Fair lord,—
Take that for your fair lady.

Please it you,
As much in private, and I'll bid adieu.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: What, was your vizard made without a tongue?

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text: I know the reason, lady, why you ask.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: O for your reason! quickly, sir; I long.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: O for your reason! quickly, sir; I long.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text:
You have a double tongue within your mask,
And would afford my speechless vizard half.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: Veal, quoth the Dutchman. Is not "veal" a calf?

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text: A calf, fair lady!

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: No, a fair lord calf.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text: Let's part the word.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text:
No, I'll not be your half
Take all, and wean it; it may prove an ox.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text:
No, I'll not be your half
Take all, and wean it; it may prove an ox.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text:
Look, how you butt yourself in these sharp mocks!
Will you give horns, chaste lady? do not so.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text:

Will you give horns, chaste lady? do not so.

sentences.text:

Will you give horns, chaste lady? do not so.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: Then die a calf, before your horns do grow.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text: One word in private with you, ere I die.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: Bleat softly then; the butcher hears you cry.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: Bleat softly then; the butcher hears you cry.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
The tongues of mocking wenches are as keen
As is the razor's edge invisible,
Cutting a smaller hair than may be seen,
Above the sense of sense; so sensible
Seemeth their conference; their conceits have wings
Fleeter than arrows, bullets, wind, thought, swifter
things.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: Not one word more, my maids; break off, break off.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: Not one word more, my maids; break off, break off.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: By heaven, all dry-beaten with pure scoff!

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Farewell, mad wenches; you have simple wits.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Twenty adieus, my frozen Muscovits.
Exeunt FERDINAND, Lords, and Blackamoors

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Are these the breed of wits so wonder'd at?

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: Tapers they are, with your sweet breaths puff'd out.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: Well-liking wits they have; gross, gross; fat, fat.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
O poverty in wit, kingly-poor flout!
Will they not, think you, hang themselves tonight?
Or ever, but in vizards, show their faces?
This pert Biron was out of countenance quite.

sentences.text:
O, they were all in lamentable cases!
The king was weeping-ripe for a good word.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Biron did swear himself out of all suit.

sentences.speaker: MARIA
sentences.text:
Dumain was at my service, and his sword:
Non point, quoth I; my servant straight was mute.

sentences.speaker: MARIA
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text:
Lord Longaville said, I came o'er his heart;
And trow you what he called me?

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Qualm, perhaps.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: Yes, in good faith.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Go, sickness as thou art!

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
Well, better wits have worn plain statute-caps.
But will you hear? the king is my love sworn.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:

But will you hear? the king is my love sworn.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: And quick Biron hath plighted faith to me.

sentences.speaker: KATHARINE
sentences.text: And Longaville was for my service born.

sentences.speaker: MARIA
sentences.text: Dumain is mine, as sure as bark on tree.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
Madam, and pretty mistresses, give ear:
Immediately they will again be here
In their own shapes; for it can never be
They will digest this harsh indignity.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
Madam, and pretty mistresses, give ear:

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Will they return?

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
They will, they will, God knows,
And leap for joy, though they are lame with blows:
Therefore change favours; and, when they repair,
Blow like sweet roses in this summer air.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
PRINCESS
sentences.text:
Avaunt, perplexity! What shall we do,
If they return in their own shapes to woo?

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
Avaunt, perplexity! What shall we do,
If they return in their own shapes to woo?

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
Good madam, if by me you'll be advised,
Let's, mock them still, as well known as disguised:
Let us complain to them what fools were here,
Disguised like Muscovites, in shapeless gear;
And wonder what they were and to what end
Their shallow shows and prologue vilely penn'd
And their rough carriage so ridiculous,
Should be presented at our tent to us.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Fair sir, God save you! Where's the princess?

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Fair sir, God save you! Where's the princess?

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
Gone to her tent. Please it your majesty
Command me any service to her thither?

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: That she vouchsafe me audience for one word.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: I will; and so will she, I know, my lord.
Exit

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: All hail, sweet madam, and fair time of day!

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: "Fair" in "all hail" is foul, as I conceive.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Construe my speeches better, if you may.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Then wish me better; I will give you leave.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
We came to visit you, and purpose now
To lead you to our court; vouchsafe it then.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:

To lead you to our court; vouchsafe it then.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
This field shall hold me; and so hold your vow:
Nor God, nor I, delights in perjured men.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
This field shall hold me; and so hold your vow:
Nor God, nor I, delights in perjured men.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
Rebuke me not for that which you provoke:
The virtue of your eye must break my oath.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
Rebuke me not for that which you provoke:
The virtue of your eye must break my oath.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
You nickname virtue; vice you should have spoke;
For virtue's office never breaks men's troth.
Now by my maiden honour, yet as pure
As the unsullied lily, I protest,
A world of torments though I should endure,
I would not yield to be your house's guest;
So much I hate a breaking cause to be
Of heavenly oaths, vow'd with integrity.

O, you have lived in desolation here,
Unseen, unvisited, much to our shame.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
Ay, in truth, my lord;
Trim gallants, full of courtship and of state.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
Madam, speak true. It is not so, my lord:
My lady, to the manner of the days,
In courtesy gives undeserving praise.
We four indeed confronted were with four
In Russian habit: here they stay'd an hour,
And talk'd apace; and in that hour, my lord,
They did not bless us with one happy word.
I dare not call them fools; but this I think,
When they are thirsty, fools would fain have drink.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
This jest is dry to me. Fair gentle sweet,
Your wit makes wise things foolish: when we greet,
With eyes best seeing, heaven's fiery eye,
By light we lose light: your capacity
Is of that nature that to your huge store
Wise things seem foolish and rich things but poor.

sentences.text: This proves you wise and rich, for in my eye,—

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: I am a fool, and full of poverty.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
But that you take what doth to you belong,
It were a fault to snatch words from my tongue.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: O, I am yours, and all that I possess!

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text: All the fool mine?

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: I cannot give you less.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
There, then, that vizard; that superfluous case
That hid the worse and show'd the better face.

sentences.text: We are descried; they'll mock us now downright.

sentences.text: Amazed, my lord? why looks your highness sad?

Help, hold his brows! he'll swoon! Why look you pale?
Sea-sick, I think, coming from Muscovy.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Thus pour the stars down plagues for perjury.
Can any face of brass hold longer out? Here stand I,
lady, dart thy skill at me; Bruise me
with scorn, confound me with a flout;
Thrust thy sharp wit quite through my ignorance;
Cut me to pieces with thy keen conceit;
And I will wish thee never more to dance,
Nor never more in Russian habit wait.
O, never will I trust to speeches penn'd,
Nor to the motion of a schoolboy's tongue,
Nor never come in vizard to my friend,
Nor woo in rhyme, like a blind harper's song!
Taffeta phrases, silken terms precise,
Three-piled hyperboles, spruce affectation,
Figures pedantical; these summer-flies
Have blown me full of maggot ostentation:
I do forswear them; and I here protest,
By this white glove;—how white the hand, God knows!—
Henceforth my wooing mind shall be express'd
In russet yeas and honest kersey noes:
And, to begin, wench,—so God help me, la!—
My love to thee is sound, sans crack or flaw.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
Teach us, sweet madam, for our rude transgression
Some fair excuse.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
The fairest is confession.
Were not you here but even now disguised?

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
The fairest is confession.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Madam, I was.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: And were you well advised?

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: I was, fair madam.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
When you then were here,
What did you whisper in your lady's ear?

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: That more than all the world I did respect her.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: When she shall challenge this, you will reject her.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS

Peace, peace! forbear:
Your oath once broke, you force not to forswear.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS

I will: and therefore keep it. Rosaline,
What did the Russian whisper in your ear?

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
Madam, he swore that he did hold me dear
As precious eyesight, and did value me
Above this world; adding thereto moreover
That he would wed me, or else die my lover.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
God give thee joy of him! the noble lord
Most honourably doth unhold his word.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
What mean you, madam? by my life, my troth,
I never swore this lady such an oath.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
What mean you, madam? by my life, my troth,
I never swore this lady such an oath.

sentences.speaker: ROSALINE
sentences.text:
By heaven, you did; and to confirm it plain,
You gave me this: but take it, sir, again.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
My faith and this the princess I did give:
I knew her by this jewel on her sleeve.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:

BIRON
sentences.text:
Neither of either; I remit both twain.
I see the trick on't: here was a consent,
Knowing aforehand of our merriment,
To dash it like a Christmas comedy:
Some carry-tale, some please-man, some slight zany,
Some mumble-news, some trencher-knight, some Dick,
That smiles his cheek in years and knows the trick
To make my lady laugh when she's disposed,
Told our intents before; which once disclosed,
The ladies did change favours: and then we,
Following the signs, woo'd but the sign of she.
Now, to our perjury to add more terror,
We are again forsworn, in will and error.
Much upon this it is: and might not you.

Neither of either; I remit both twain.
I see the trick on't: here was a consent,
Knowing aforehand of our merriment,
To dash it like a Christmas comedy:
Some carry-tale, some please-man, some slight zany,
Some mumble-news, some trencher-knight, some Dick,
That smiles his cheek in years and knows the trick
To make my lady laugh when she's disposed,
Told our intents before; which once disclosed,
The ladies did change favours: and then we,
Following the signs, woo'd but the sign of she.
Now, to our perjury to add more terror,
We are again forsworn, in will and error.
Much upon this it is: and might not you.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Forestall our sport, to make us thus untrue?
Do not you know my lady's foot by the squier,
And laugh upon the apple of her eye?
And stand between her back, sir, and the fire,
Holding a trencher, jesting merrily?
You put our page out: go, you are allow'd;
Die when you will, a smock shall be your shroud.
You leer upon me, do you? there's an eye
Wounds like a leaden sword.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
Full merrily
Hath this brave manage, this career, been run.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Lo, he is tilting straight! Peace! I have done.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Lo, he is tilting straight! Peace! I have done.
Enter COSTARD

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Welcome, pure wit! thou partest a fair fray.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Welcome, pure wit! thou partest a fair fray.

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
O Lord, sir, they would know
Whether the three Worthies shall come in or no.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: What, are there but three?

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: What, are there but three?

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
No, sir; but it is vara fine,
For every one pursents three.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: And three times thrice is nine.

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
Not so, sir; under correction, sir; I hope it is not
so.
You cannot beg us, sir, I can assure you, sir we know
what we know:
I hope, sir, three times thrice, sir,—

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
Not so, sir; under correction, sir; I hope it is not
so.
You cannot beg us, sir, I can assure you, sir we know
what we know:
I hope, sir, three times thrice, sir,—

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
Not so, sir; under correction, sir; I hope it is not
so.
You cannot beg us, sir, I can assure you, sir we know
what we know:
I hope, sir, three times thrice, sir,—

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Is not nine.

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
Under correction, sir, we know whereuntil it doth
amount.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: By Jove, I always took three threes for nine.

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
O Lord, sir, it were pity you should get your living
by reckoning, sir.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: How much is it?

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
O Lord, sir, the parties themselves, the actors,
sir, will show whereuntil it doth amount: for mine
own part, I am, as they say, but to parfect one man
in one poor man, Pompion the Great, sir.

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
O Lord, sir, the parties themselves, the actors,
sir, will show whereuntil it doth amount: for mine
own part, I am, as they say, but to parfect one man
in one poor man, Pompion the Great, sir.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Art thou one of the Worthies?

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
It pleased them to think me worthy of Pompion the
Great: for mine own part, I know not the degree of
the Worthy, but I am to stand for him.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Go, bid them prepare.

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
We will turn it finely off, sir; we will take
some care.
Exit

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Biron, they will shame us: let them not approach.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
We are shame-proof, my lord: and tis some policy
To have one show worse than the king's and his company.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: I say they shall not come.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text:
Nay, my good lord, let me o'errule you now:
That sport best pleases that doth least know how:
Where zeal strives to content, and the contents
Dies in the zeal of that which it presents:
Their form confounded makes most form in mirth,
When great things labouring perish in their birth.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: A right description of our sport, my lord.
Enter DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
sentences.text:
Anointed, I implore so much expense of thy royal
sweet breath as will utter a brace of words.

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
sentences.text:
Anointed, I implore so much expense of thy royal
sweet breath as will utter a brace of words.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Doth this man serve God?

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Why ask you?

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: He speaks not like a man of God's making.

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
sentences.text:
That is all one, my fair, sweet, honey monarch; for,
I protest, the schoolmaster is exceeding
fantastical; too, too vain, too too vain: but we
will put it, as they say, to fortuna de la guerra.
I wish you the peace of mind, most royal couplement!

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text:
Here is like to be a good presence of Worthies. He
presents Hector of Troy; the swain, Pompey the
Great; the parish curate, Alexander; Armado's page,
Hercules; the pedant, Judas Maccabaeus: And if
these four Worthies in their first show thrive,
These four will change habits, and present the other
five.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: You are deceived; 'tis not so.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
The pedant, the braggart, the hedge-priest, the fool
and the boy:—
Abate throw at novum, and the whole world again
Cannot pick out five such, take each one in his vein.

Enter COSTARD, for Pompey

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text: I Pompey am,—

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: You lie, you are not he.

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text: I Pompey am,—

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: With leopard's head on knee.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Well said, old mocker: I must needs be friends
with thee.

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text: I Pompey am, Pompey surnamed the Big—

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: The Great.

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
It is, 'Great,' sir:—
Pompey surnamed the Great;
That oft in field, with targe and shield, did make
my foe to sweat:
And travelling along this coast, I here am come by
chance,
And lay my arms before the legs of this sweet lass of
France,
If your ladyship would say, 'Thanks, Pompey,' I had
done.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Great thanks, great Pompey.

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
'Tis not so much worth; but I hope I was perfect: I
made a little fault in 'Great.'

sentences.speaker: COSTARD

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: My hat to a halfpenny, Pompey proves the best Worthy.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: My hat to a halfpenny, Pompey proves the best Worthy.

sentences.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
sentences.text:
When in the world I lived, I was the world's
commander;
By east, west, north, and south, I spread my
conquering might:
My scutcheon plain declares that I am Alisander,—

sentences.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
sentences.text:
When in the world I lived, I was the world's
commander;
By east, west, north, and south, I spread my
conquering might:
My scutcheon plain declares that I am Alisander,—

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
Your nose says, no, you are not for it stands too
right.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Your nose smells 'no' in this, most tender-smelling
knight.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: The conqueror is dismay'd. Proceed, good Alexander.

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: The conqueror is dismay'd. Proceed, good Alexander.

sentences.speaker: SIR NATHANIEL
sentences.text:
When in the world I lived, I was the world's
commander,—

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: Most true, 'tis right; you were so, Alisander.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Pompey the Great,—

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text: Your servant, and Costard.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Take away the conqueror, take away Alisander.

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
[To SIR NATHANIEL] O, sir, you have overthrown
Alisander the conqueror! You will be scraped out of
the painted cloth for this: your lion, that holds
his poll-axe sitting on a close-stool, will be given
to Ajax: he will be the ninth Worthy. A conqueror,
and afeard to speak! run away for shame, Alisander.

sentences.speaker: COSTARD
sentences.text:
There, an't shall please you; a foolish mild man; an
honest man, look you, and soon dashed. He is a
marvellous good neighbour, faith, and a very good
bowler: but, for Alisander,—alas, you see how
'tis,—a little o'erparted. But there are Worthies
a-coming will speak their mind in some other sort.

Enter HOLOFERNES, for Judas; and MOTH, for Hercules

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
Great Hercules is presented by this imp,
Whose club kill'd Cerberus, that three-headed canis;
And when he was a babe, a child, a shrimp,
Thus did he strangle serpents in his manus.
Quoniam he seemeth in minority,
Ergo I come with this apology.
Keep some state in thy exit, and vanish.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: Judas I am,—

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: A Judas!

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text:
Not Iscariot, sir.
Judas I am, ycliped Maccabaeus.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: Judas Maccabaeus clipt is plain Judas.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: A kissing traitor. How art thou proved Judas?

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: A kissing traitor. How art thou proved Judas?

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: Judas I am,—

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: The more shame for you, Judas.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: What mean you, sir?

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: To make Judas hang himself.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: Begin, sir; you are my elder.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Well followed: Judas was hanged on an elder.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: I will not be put out of countenance.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Because thou hast no face.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: What is this?

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: A cittern-head.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: The head of a bodkin.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: A Death's face in a ring.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text: The face of an old Roman coin, scarce seen.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: The pommel of Caesar's falchion.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: The carved-bone face on a flask.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Saint George's half-cheek in a brooch.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: Ay, and in a brooch of lead.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Ay, and worn in the cap of a tooth-drawer.
And now forward; for we have put thee in countenance.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text:
Ay, and worn in the cap of a tooth-drawer.
And now forward; for we have put thee in countenance.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: You have put me out of countenance.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: False; we have given thee faces.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: False; we have given thee faces.

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: But you have out-faced them all.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: An thou wert a lion, we would do so.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
Therefore, as he is an ass, let him go.
And so adieu, sweet Jude! nay, why dost thou stay?

sentences.text:

sentences.text: For the latter end of his name.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: For the ass to the Jude; give it him:—Jud-as, away!

sentences.speaker: HOLOFERNES
sentences.text: This is not generous, not gentle, not humble.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
A light for Monsieur Judas! it grows dark, he may
stumble.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text:
A light for Monsieur Judas! it grows dark, he may
stumble.
HOLOFERNES retires

sentences.speaker: PRINCESS
sentences.text: Alas, poor Maccabaeus, how hath he been baited!
Enter DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO, for Hector

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: Hide thy head, Achilles: here comes Hector in arms.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: Though my mocks come home by me, I will now be merry.

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: Hector was but a Troyan in respect of this.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: But is this Hector?

sentences.speaker: FERDINAND
sentences.text: I think Hector was not so clean-timbered.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text: His leg is too big for Hector's.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: More calf, certain.

sentences.speaker: BOYET
sentences.text: No; he is best endued in the small.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: This cannot be Hector.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: He's a god or a painter; for he makes faces.

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
sentences.text:
The armipotent Mars, of lances the almighty,
Gave Hector a gift,—

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: A gilt nutmeg.

sentences.speaker: BIRON
sentences.text: A lemon.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text: Stuck with cloves.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: No, cloven.

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
sentences.text:
Peace!—
The armipotent Mars, of lances the almighty
Gave Hector a gift, the heir of Ilion;
A man so breathed, that certain he would fight; yea
From morn till night, out of his pavilion.
I am that flower,—

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
sentences.text:

Gave Hector a gift, the heir of Ilion;

I am that flower,—

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: That mint.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text: That columbine.

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
sentences.text: Sweet Lord Longaville, rein thy tongue.

sentences.speaker: LONGAVILLE
sentences.text:
I must rather give it the rein, for it runs against
Hector.

sentences.speaker: DUMAIN
sentences.text: Ay, and Hector's a greyhound.

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
sentences.text:
The sweet war-man is dead and rotten; sweet chucks,
beat not the bones of the buried: when he breathed,
he was a man. But I will forward with my device.

sentences.speaker: DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO
sentences.text:
The sweet war-man is dead and rotten; sweet chucks,
beat not the bones of the buried: when he breathed,
he was a man. But I will forward with my device.

sentences.text:
The sweet war-man is dead and rotten; sweet chucks,
beat not the bones of the buried: when he breathed,
he was a man. But I will forward with my device.

sentences.text:
The sweet war-man is dead and rotten; sweet chucks,
beat not the bones of the buried: when he breathed,
